title: Dashboards + alerts for tiered batching metrics (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
preview:
Goal
Implement dashboards and alerts for tiered batching defaults (fast / standard / cost-efficient) per the Confluence spec. Primary outcomes:
- Make tier behavior visible (latency, queueing, batching effectiveness, overload mode) broken down by tier/model/region.
- Provide actionable alerting that catches regressions early without paging noise.
- Ensure telemetry/cardinality is controlled and costs stay bounded during rollout.

Scope
- Dashboards:
  - Latency (p50/p95/p99) time series + histograms by tier (fast/standard/cost-efficient), model family, region.
  - Streaming-specific latency panels (TTFT + per-chunk cadence where available) for fast tier.
  - Scheduler health: queue depth, queue wait time, admission/backpressure events, load-shed rate.
  - Batching effectiveness: batch size distribution (prefill/decode), max_batch_tokens utilization, max_wait_ms utilization.
  - Cache: prefix/KV cache hit rate (where supported) + impact on TTFT.
  - Profile selection: reason codes (default vs override vs model constraint vs overload mode) and override prevalence.
  - Error rates and saturation: 429/503 rates, GPU utilization, OOM/evictions (where instrumented).

- Alerts:
  - Fast tier tail latency regression (primary): p99 and p95 SLO burn alerts with appropriate windows.
  - Standard tier p95 regression (secondary): ticket-first; page only on sustained burn.
  - Cost-efficient tier queue blow-up/overload: queue depth + wait time + shed rate; ticket-first unless severe.
  - Telemetry guardrail: cardinality / label explosion detection (protect telemetry spend + stability).

References
- Confluence dashboard/alert spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/batching-defaults-observability-dashboard-spec.json
- Metrics taxonomy draft (labels/cardinality): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/batching-defaults-metrics-taxonomy.json
- SLO tiers definition: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/latency-slo-tiers-definition-fast-standard-cost-efficient.json
- Related implementation PRs:
  - observability-pack: sources/github/observability-pack/pr-933-add-tiered-batching-dashboards.json
  - runtime metric labels + cardinality controls: sources/github/redwood/pr-28492-runtime-metrics-tier-labels-and-cardinality-controls.json
  - hotfix for tier label cardinality: sources/github/redwood/pr-28544-hotfix-limit-tier-label-cardinality.json
- Related tickets:
  - ENG-18426 Add reason codes for profile selection
  - ENG-18428 Perf canary gate on tiered SLO
  - INT-33012 Dashboard missing tier breakdown labels (follow-up validation)

Decisions / alerting philosophy
- Default stance: avoid paging for noisy / action-unclear signals.
- Paging is reserved for: fast-tier sustained p99 burn OR broad elevated 5xx/429 correlated with queue blowups.
- Prefer multi-signal alerts for overload (queue depth + wait + shed) to reduce false positives.
- Use burn-rate style alerts (fast tier):
  - Page on fast burn (e.g., 2% error budget in 1h) and slow burn (e.g., 5% in 6h) to catch both spikes and drifts.
  - Gate pages to routes/regions under canary rollout flag when possible.
- Ticket alerts for: standard tier p95 drift, cost-efficient tier queue increases, override prevalence anomalies.

Implementation notes
- Dashboard dimensions
  - Required labels: tier, model, region.
  - Optional labels (strictly controlled): route (sampled/top-N), dedicated_cluster (dedicated only, allowlist), tenant (NOT allowed).
  - Reason codes aggregated (do NOT label high-cardinality freeform strings).
- Cardinality controls
  - Tier label added to core latency metrics; route/model breakdown only on curated allowlist.
  - Added drop/aggregation rules for unexpected label values.
  - Added telemetry spend/ingestion panels and alerts.

Acceptance criteria
- Dashboards
  - New "Tiered batching" dashboard is live in Observability Pack and accessible from the runtime on-call landing page.
  - Panels render in <10s for 24h window (no unbounded cardinality queries).
  - Includes "Canary" filter and clearly indicates rollout cohort (flag state) where available.
- Alerts
  - Fast-tier p99 regression pages only when sustained (validated on historical replays / staging load tests).
  - Standard/cost-efficient alerts create tickets by default with runbook links.
  - All alerts include: tier, model family, region, top suspected cause hints (queueing vs errors vs overload mode), and links to the dashboard.
- Runbooks
  - Alerts link to ENG-18433 runbook section (overload behavior + rollback/killswitch) and the rollout checklist.

Rollout plan
- Phase 1: ship dashboards (no paging) + ticket alerts in staging; verify filters and label health.
- Phase 2: enable paging only for fast-tier p99 burn in canary cohort; tune thresholds based on 1 week data.
- Phase 3: broaden to hosted subset + Dedicated opt-in; keep paging scope limited to fast tier.

Open questions
- Confirm whether TTFT and streaming chunk cadence metrics are uniformly available across top model families (if not, add "data coverage" panel and update runbook).
- Decide if cost-efficient tier should page on extreme queue wait only when 5xx increases (multi-signal) vs wait alone.

Notes on alert tuning (avoid noise)
- Suppress pages for <N RPS (low traffic) regions unless error rates are extreme; otherwise, create ticket.
- Add auto-resolve + cooldown periods to prevent flapping during autoscaler events.
- During rollout, tag alerts with rollout cohort; do not page on non-canary cohorts until thresholds are validated.

Deliverables
- observability-pack dashboard + alert rules merged (PR-933).
- Tier label panels validated post-hotfix (PR-28544).
- Links added to runtime on-call landing page and rollout checklist.

title: Incidents runbook: overload behavior with tiered batching defaults (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
preview:
## Goal
Provide a single on-call runbook for diagnosing and mitigating overload-related latency regressions when tiered batching defaults are enabled (fast / standard / cost-efficient). This runbook covers:
- symptom → likely cause mapping
- immediate mitigations (no code)
- tier-specific guidance
- rollback / kill-switch procedures
- post-incident follow-ups

This is intended for Runtime + SRE on-call during staged rollout (internal canary → hosted subset → Dedicated opt-in → broader default).

---

## Definitions / mental model
**Overload** here means one (or more) of:
- arrival rate > sustainable throughput for the active tier profile
- queue growth that increases time-to-first-token (TTFT) and/or p99 end-to-end latency
- admission control/load shedding triggers (or fails to trigger)
- scheduler behavior that starves some requests under mixed prompt length / mixed streaming

**Tier expectations** (high-level):
- **fast**: protect tail latency; will sacrifice throughput earlier via tighter wait budget + admission control.
- **standard**: balanced; modest batching, modest wait; aims to be stable for general SaaS workloads.
- **cost-efficient**: maximize throughput / $ per token; can tolerate higher tail, but must not allow runaway queue depth.

**Key guarantee:** correctness must be preserved; degrade predictably under overload (backpressure, rate limiting, or load shedding) rather than silent latency collapse.

---

## When to use this runbook
Trigger when any of the following occur after enabling tiered defaults (feature flag on, or tier selection introduced):
1) **Fast tier p99 regression** (especially TTFT) vs baseline.
2) **Queue depth increasing** with p95/p99 latency increasing.
3) **Streaming endpoints** report slower TTFT or irregular chunking (perceived "stalling").
4) Increased **429/503** (load shedding / admission control) after enabling fast tier.
5) Dedicated tenant reports override not applying or unexpected effective tier.

Related tickets/threads to reference:
- Slack: fast-tier p99 regression investigation (eng-runtime/1768501140)
- Slack: overload mode behavior discussion (eng-runtime/1768664305)
- Jira: SUP-48219 p95 regression during canary
- Hotfix precedent: PR-28544 limit tier label cardinality (telemetry blow-up can masquerade as perf regression)

---

## Required dashboards / queries
1) **Tiered batching dashboard** (Observability Pack)
- latency: p50/p95/p99 by {tier, model_family, region, streaming}
- TTFT (streaming vs non-streaming)
- queue depth and time-in-queue
- batch size distribution (prefill batch tokens, decode batch tokens)
- admission control / shed counters
- cache hit rate (prefix/KV) and cache evictions

2) **Reason codes** for profile selection
Check selection reason counts over time to confirm expected behavior.
Common reason codes:
- `tier.default.fast|standard|cost_efficient`
- `tier.override.route_policy`
- `tier.override.customer` (Dedicated)
- `tier.constrained.model_limit` (model/hardware constraint clamps knobs)
- `tier.overload_mode.active` (scheduler entered overload behavior)
- `tier.fallback.compat_variant` (if applicable)

3) **Infra saturation**
- GPU utilization / SM occupancy
- HBM usage / OOM events
- CPU saturation in control plane / streaming gateway
- network egress saturation for streaming-heavy regions

---

## First 5 minutes: decision tree
### A) Is this a telemetry artifact?
- If latency histograms degrade while service-level sampling changes or metrics cardinality spikes, verify metrics ingestion health.
- Check whether tier labels exploded (known early canary issue); if so, apply telemetry hotfix / sampling controls and re-evaluate.

### B) Is the tier actually applied?
- Confirm effective tier for affected routes/tenants.
- Look at reason code mix:
  - if `tier.override.*` dominates unexpectedly → config precedence issue.
  - if `tier.constrained.model_limit` spikes → model/hardware clamp causing different behavior than expected.

### C) Is it localized?
- Compare by region/model family. If one region/model family only, suspect:
  - heterogeneous fleet hardware
  - different concurrency distribution
  - cache behavior differences
  - a single model family with prefill/decode imbalance sensitivity

### D) Is it overload vs regression under normal load?
- If queue depth and time-in-queue increase in parallel with latency → overload/backpressure problem.
- If queue depth is flat but p99 increases → scheduler/kernel regression, model-level issue, or downstream gateway.

---

## Triage steps (deeper)
1) **Identify impacted dimension(s)**
- tier (fast vs standard vs cost-efficient)
- streaming vs non-streaming
- prompt length bucket (short/medium/long)
- model family + quantization variant (if applicable)
- region

2) **Check queue dynamics**
- queue depth trend
- max wait budget usage (esp. fast tier)
- batch formation rate (are we forming too-small batches due to tight wait? or too-large due to aggressive batching?)

3) **Check for starvation under mixed workloads**
Signals:
- long prompts are consistently delayed behind short prompts (or vice versa)
- streaming requests get delayed behind large prefill batches
Mitigation options (no-code):
- shift affected route from cost-efficient → standard, or standard → fast (depending on symptom)
- temporarily cap max batch tokens / max wait via override if available (Dedicated/enterprise)

4) **Check admission control / shedding**
- Rising 429/503 with stable latency may indicate fast tier protecting tail correctly, but upstream rate limits need tuning.
- Rising latency without shedding indicates admission control not triggering when it should (regression or thresholds too lenient).

5) **Confirm cache behavior**
- If cache hit rate dropped coincident with tier change, larger batches may be increasing eviction or changing request interleaving.
- Cache issues can present as sudden prefill time increase (TTFT regression) without obvious queue growth.

---

## Tier-specific guidance
### Fast tier (protect tail latency)
Typical failure modes:
- **p99 TTFT regression** due to queueing from insufficient admission control or too much prefill work per step.
- **increased 429/503** because admission control became more aggressive than expected.

Mitigations:
1) If p99 is rising and queue depth is rising:
- enable/raise fast-tier admission control (if not already) and verify it is active.
- reduce exposure: roll back fast tier defaults for affected model/region cohort.
2) If 429/503 rose sharply but latency stayed healthy:
- validate upstream rate limiting configs; communicate expected behavior (fast tier chooses fail-fast under overload).
- consider shifting high-QPS non-critical routes to standard tier.

Escalate to Runtime if:
- starvation suspected (mixed prompt lengths) despite admission control.
- prefill/decode balancing looks off (high prefill time spikes while decode steady).


### Standard tier (balanced)
Typical failure modes:
- slow creep in p95 under sustained load due to queue depth increasing.
- mixed streaming and non-streaming contention.

Mitigations:
- if streaming routes are impacted, isolate by moving streaming routes to fast tier.
- if batch sizes are too small (low throughput), consider cost-efficient for non-latency-sensitive routes.
- if batch sizes are too large (queue blowups), temporarily clamp max batch tokens via route override (if available) or move to fast.


### Cost-efficient tier (throughput-first)
Typical failure modes:
- **queue blowup** under bursty traffic (tail latency becomes unbounded).
- **timeouts** at gateways due to slow TTFT.

Mitigations:
1) For queue blowup:
- shift impacted route(s) to standard temporarily.
- if overload mode exists, verify it is active and is reducing queue growth (not increasing).
2) For widespread impact:
- reduce rollout scope for cost-efficient tier; disable for streaming endpoints by policy.

Hard rule during incident:
- Do not recommend cost-efficient tier for customer-facing interactive streaming endpoints unless they explicitly accept TTFT risk.

---

## Rollback / kill switch procedures
### Scope options (prefer smallest blast radius first)
1) **Per-route tier override** (Console/policy): change tier for a single route.
2) **Per-tenant override** (Dedicated): enforce tier at tenant/cluster level; ensure precedence (customer override > route policy > tier default).
3) **Disable tiered defaults feature flag** for cohort (region/model family).
4) **Global kill switch**: revert to pre-1.22 manual batching behavior / previous defaults.

### Rollback checklist
- capture incident timestamp window + impacted dimensions (tier/model/region/streaming)
- snapshot key graphs: p99, TTFT, queue depth, batch size distribution, shed counters
- confirm reason codes show the expected effective tier before and after rollback
- post rollback: monitor for 30 minutes (or 2x traffic peak window) and confirm metrics return to baseline band

### Communication
- If customer-facing degradation (Hosted): notify Support + PM on-call with:
  - affected regions/models
  - mitigation (tier shift / rollback)
  - expected time to stabilize
- If Dedicated tenant: coordinate with CSM/Support; document effective tier and override precedence.

---

## Known pitfalls / lessons learned (add as we learn)
1) **Metric cardinality can look like a perf regression**
If tier labels are added without cardinality controls, ingestion pressure can create noisy dashboards and delayed data.
Reference: runtime hotfix PR-28544.

2) **Streaming requires TTFT-first thinking**
Even if end-to-end p95 is stable, TTFT regression is a user-visible incident for streaming chat.

3) **Mixed prompt lengths can induce starvation**
Watch for bimodal latency by prompt-length bucket; consider policy split by route type.

---

## Artifacts / links
- Confluence (design): workload-aware batching defaults design doc (eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling)
- Confluence (SLOs): latency SLO tiers definition fast/standard/cost-efficient
- Confluence (dashboards): batching defaults observability dashboard spec
- GitHub: PR-28480 (feature flag + kill switch plumbing)
- GitHub: PR-28473 (reason codes)
- GitHub: observability-pack PR-933 (tier dashboards)

---

## Acceptance criteria
- Runbook published and linked from:
  - SRE on-call handbook
  - runtime-1.22 launch brief
  - batching defaults rollout checklist
- Includes explicit rollback order + kill switch instructions.
- Includes tier-specific symptom→mitigation guidance.
- References dashboards and reason codes needed for validation.

---

## Follow-ups (not required for closure, but tracked)
- Add a lightweight "incident note template" to the rollout checklist (owner: Rafael Mendes)
- Add an alert annotation when overload mode toggles (owner: Allison Grant)
- Add dedicated override precedence troubleshooting snippet to support playbook (owner: Aisha Bello)

- confirm reason codes show the expected effective tier before and after rollback
- post rollback: monitor for 30 minutes (or 2x traffic peak window) and confirm metrics return to baseline band

### Communication
- If customer-facing degradation (Hosted): notify Support + PM on-call with:
  - affected regions/models
  - mitigation (tier shift / rollback)
  - expected time to stabilize
- If Dedicated tenant: coordinate with CSM/Support; document effective tier and override precedence.

---

## Known pitfalls / lessons learned (add as we learn)
1) **Metric cardinality can look like a perf regression**
If tier labels are added without cardinality controls, ingestion pressure can create noisy dashboards and delayed data.
Reference: runtime hotfix PR-28544.

2) **Streaming requires TTFT-first thinking**
Even if end-to-end p95 is stable, TTFT regression is a user-visible incident for streaming chat.

3) **Mixed prompt lengths can induce starvation**
Watch for bimodal latency by prompt-length bucket; consider policy split by route type.

---

## Artifacts / links
- Confluence (design): workload-aware batching defaults design doc (eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling)
- Confluence (SLOs): latency SLO tiers definition fast/standard/cost-efficient
- Confluence (dashboards): batching defaults observability dashboard spec
- GitHub: PR-28480 (feature flag + kill switch plumbing)
- GitHub: PR-28473 (reason codes)
- GitHub: observability-pack PR-933 (tier dashboards)

---

## Acceptance criteria
- Runbook published and linked from:
  - SRE on-call handbook
  - runtime-1.22 launch brief
  - batching defaults rollout checklist
- Includes explicit rollback order + kill switch instructions.
- Includes tier-specific symptom→mitigation guidance.
- References dashboards and reason codes needed for validation.

---

## Follow-ups (not required for closure, but tracked)
- Add a lightweight "incident note template" to the rollout checklist (owner: Rafael Mendes)
- Add an alert annotation when overload mode toggles (owner: Allison Grant)
- Add dedicated override precedence troubleshooting snippet to support playbook (owner: Aisha Bello)

title: Guardrails for auto-rollback: minimum sample sizes + debounce/holdoff to prevent flapping
preview:
Background
Auto-rollback/pause decisions driven by latency/error SLOs are highly sensitive to short-lived spikes, metric lag, and low-volume routes. We need explicit guardrails so the rollout controller doesn’t flap (pause/rollback/resume repeatedly) or overreact to single-sample anomalies.

This ticket implements and documents:
1) Minimum sample size gating for each evaluated SLO window (fast + slow burn).
2) Debounce/holdoff rules (consecutive breach confirmation + cooldown) to avoid oscillation.

Scope
- Add minimum request volume requirements to the SLO evaluator before an SLO window is considered “actionable”.
- Add debounce rules that require breach confirmation across multiple evaluation ticks/windows.
- Add holdoff/cooldown after an action (pause/rollback) so we don’t trigger duplicate actions due to stale evaluations or step-transition jitter.
- Emit clear audit reasons and metrics labels so oncall can tell “breach confirmed” vs “breach suspected but insufficient samples”.
- Document rationale and defaults; align with ADR-0127 and SRE recommendations.

Non-goals
- Full quality/eval regression signals.
- Changing SLO definitions/threshold defaults (handled by PM-618 / SRE docs).

title: Add drop-rate + delivery latency alerts for audit log pipeline (with on-call routing + runbook links)
preview:
## Context
Audit logging is moving toward 100% coverage for sensitive actions across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. We need alerting that makes audit pipeline failures obvious and actionable (drop rate + excessive delivery latency), with correct paging/routing and clear runbook references.

This ticket is focused on alert rules + routing + linking to the incident runbook (not on building new pipeline features). It should be compatible with both Hosted and Private deployments, acknowledging that some Private customers forward to their own SIEM and may not run our full managed stack.

## Goals
1) Detect audit event loss (true drops) quickly.
2) Detect sustained high delivery latency (backlog) quickly.
3) Route alerts to the correct on-call (SRE platform) with links to dashboards + runbooks.
4) Avoid alert noise during known failure modes (expected retries, planned maintenance), while still paging on sustained customer-impacting conditions.

## Definitions / signals
We care about two stages:
- Ingestion: producer -> audit ingestion service / queue
- Delivery: ingestion -> storage/exporter (and exporter -> external SIEM targets, where applicable)

### Drop rate (loss)
Primary: ratio-based drop rate comparing "accepted" vs "persisted/forwarded" counts.
- drop_rate = 1 - (audit_events_persisted_total / audit_events_accepted_total)
- Evaluate per environment + per region + per deployment_mode (hosted/dedicated/private) where labels exist.

Fallback if ratios are unreliable: use explicit "dropped" counter where implemented.
- audit_events_dropped_total (reason label: validation_failed, payload_too_large, queue_overflow, downstream_error, unknown)

### Delivery latency (backlog)
Primary: end-to-end delivery latency histogram (event timestamp -> persisted/exported timestamp)
- audit_event_delivery_latency_seconds{percentile="p95"} or a histogram quantile derived from buckets

Secondary: queue backlog age / consumer lag (if we have Kafka/SQS style metrics)
- audit_queue_oldest_message_age_seconds
- audit_queue_consumer_lag

## Proposed alerting (initial thresholds)
We should tune after 1–2 weeks of real traffic, but need a safe baseline for enterprise readiness.

### 1) Audit pipeline drop rate (CRITICAL)
Condition:
- drop_rate > 1% for 10m
Scope:
- page SRE on-call
Notes:
- This is a "customer-impacting" page. Sustained drops break compliance expectations.

### 2) Audit pipeline drop rate (WARNING)
Condition:
- drop_rate > 0.1% for 15m
Scope:
- notify (non-page) to #eng-sre + create incident channel if trending

### 3) Audit delivery latency high (CRITICAL)
Condition:
- p95 delivery latency > 120s for 15m
Scope:
- page SRE on-call
Rationale:
- Latency in audit logs prevents timely investigations and can break promised export timeliness for some customers.

### 4) Audit delivery latency high (WARNING)
Condition:
- p95 delivery latency > 60s for 20m
Scope:
- notify #eng-sre

### 5) Validation failures spike (WARNING)
Condition:
- rate(audit_events_dropped_total{reason="validation_failed"}[10m]) > baseline + threshold
Scope:
- notify #eng-security and #eng-platform (likely schema regression / producer bug)
Notes:
- This should not page SRE by default unless it also triggers drop-rate critical.

## Routing requirements
- Paging destination: SRE Platform on-call rotation
- Slack: #eng-sre for warning-level notifications
- Include runbook link: "Audit log pipeline incident runbook" (Confluence)
- Include dashboard link: Audit pipeline dashboard (Grafana/Observability pack)
- Include "owner hints": ingestion vs exporter vs upstream-producer suspicion based on which signals fired

## Implementation notes
- Implement as Prometheus alert rules in observability-pack (or the standard alerts repo used by SRE).
- Ensure alerts include labels/annotations:
  - service, env, region, deployment_mode
  - runbook_url
  - dashboard_url
  - a short "what changed" hint (e.g., drop_rate, persisted/accepted ratio)
- Add silencing guidance:
  - maintenance windows should use standard silences (not permanent threshold bumps)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Alert rules merged and deployed to staging + prod (Hosted). Private pack should include the rules where applicable.
- [ ] Alerts route correctly:
  - warning -> Slack #eng-sre
  - critical -> SRE on-call paging policy
- [ ] Each alert includes:
  - runbook link
  - dashboard link
  - clear trigger description + query context
- [ ] We can force a synthetic failure in staging (e.g., exporter disabled / consumer paused) and verify:
  - delivery latency alert fires
  - drop rate alert does NOT false-fire for expected retry behavior unless there is actual loss
- [ ] Runbook updated (if needed) with exact alert names and first-step checks.

## Out of scope
- Building new ingestion buffering/retry mechanisms (tracked in ENG-4829).
- Creating a customer-facing audit log UI.

## Links / related work
- Observability dashboards + initial graphs: sources/github/observability-pack/pr-433-audit-log-pipeline-dashboards-and-alerts.json
- Delivery SLO definition: sources/github/slo-toolkit/pr-298-add-audit-log-delivery-slo.json
- Incident runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook.json
- Noise tuning follow-up (historical): sources/jira/internal-support/INT-3984-audit-log-pipeline-alert-noise.json

## Open questions
1) Should we split alerts per customer_tier (enterprise vs standard), or keep one set and page only when enterprise workloads are impacted?
2) For Private: do we ship these alerts in the default observability bundle, and how do we document required metrics prerequisites?
3) Confirm metric names + labels are stable post schema v1 rollout (coordinate with Logan/Nadia telemetry changes).

title: On-call runbook + readiness for auto-rollback on SLO breach
preview:
Background
Auto-rollback/pause on SLO breach is landing behind a feature flag. Before enabling for any production routes, we need on-call ready documentation, alert routing, and a short training package so responders can (a) trust the automation, (b) quickly validate whether it’s correct, and (c) safely override when needed.

Scope
1) Runbook updates (primary)
- Publish/refresh the SRE runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENG/Auto-rollback+on+SLO+breach+%28on-call%29
  - How to interpret the auto action (pause vs rollback) and what it changes in traffic routing.
  - Where to find the decision trace + metrics snapshot (Console + audit logs).
  - Triage flow: confirm scope (route/model/region/tier), confirm monitor freshness, confirm sample size gating, check burn-rate windows, validate rollback executor status.
  - Failure modes + what to do:
    - monitor missing/stale (expected behavior + how to confirm)
    - false positives / flapping
    - rollback executor partial failure / idempotency
    - telemetry outage (suppression guidance)
  - Override/suppression steps (admin-only): when allowed, how long, required justification, and how to revert.
  - Safe “re-attempt rollout” checklist after fix.

2) Incident playbook alignment
- Add a short section to the incident playbook page on when to rely on auto-rollback vs manual mitigation:
  https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/IC/rollout-incident-playbook-auto-mitigation
- Ensure comms expectations are explicit (what gets posted to Slack, what pages, when to escalate to service owners).

3) Dashboards + alert links
- Ensure the runbook links to the canonical Grafana dashboards and alert definitions:
  - “Auto-rollback: Overview (All services)” (owner: Aisha)
  - “Rollouts: Health by route/model/region”
  - “SLO Monitor freshness / staleness”
- Confirm alert routing is documented:
  - Which alerts page vs notify-only
  - Target Slack channels and on-call rotations

4) Training / on-call readiness
- Deliver a lightweight training package:
  - 30-minute live walkthrough for ENG-SRE + platform on-call
  - 1-page checklist for “first 10 minutes” response
  - Tabletop scenario: canary latency spike + monitor staleness
- Track attendance + ensure new responders can find the doc.

Non-goals
- Defining new SLO thresholds (owned by PM-618 / SRE semantics doc)
- Building new UI surfaces (Console work tracked elsewhere)

Acceptance criteria
- Runbook is published, reviewed by SRE lead, and linked from the internal announcement.
- Runbook includes: triage flow, override steps, and a “re-attempt rollout” checklist.
- Runbook links to dashboards + alert rules; links verified to be accessible by on-call groups.
- Training completed and recording/link posted.
- On-call readiness sign-off captured in comments (Sean + Aisha).

title: Post auto-rollback events to Slack via incident-bot (dedupe + rate limit to avoid paging storms)
preview:
Context
Auto-rollback/pause decisions are now emitted as structured audit events and surfaced in Console. On-call needs a fast, low-noise notification path when an auto action occurs, without creating paging storms (e.g., flapping rollouts, rapid step retries, or multiple regions firing simultaneously).

Goal
Update incident-bot to post concise Slack notifications for auto actions (rollback/pause/suppress + missing/stale monitor) with:
- consistent formatting
- links to the relevant dashboards + Console rollout view
- dedupe + rate limiting per rollout_id/service to prevent alert floods
- clear ownership routing (service channel vs oncall channel) without paging by default

Non-goals
- PagerDuty integration for auto-rollback (we will revisit after we have stable noise characteristics).
- Full incident auto-creation in Slack (separate workstream).

Event sources
- Consume rollout auto-action audit events from the event stream (emitted by redwood backend).
- Primary event types to handle:
  1) rollout.auto_action.executed (action=ROLLBACK|PAUSE|SHIFT_TO_BASELINE)
  2) rollout.auto_action.suppressed (reason=ADMIN_OVERRIDE|KNOWN_INCIDENT|INSUFFICIENT_SAMPLE|DEBOUNCE|STALE_MONITOR)
  3) rollout.slo_monitor.missing_or_stale

Slack delivery requirements
1) Channels
- Default: #eng-oncall (notify) for executed actions (ROLLBACK/PAUSE/SHIFT_TO_BASELINE).
- Secondary routing to service owner channel if known (config map in incident-bot): e.g., #eng-platform-rollouts, #runtime-oncall, etc.
- Suppressed + missing/stale monitor: post to #eng-sre (notify) (not #eng-oncall) unless suppression reason is "KNOWN_INCIDENT" (then do nothing to avoid noise during active incident).

2) Message format (single message per event)
Include:
- Action + short reason (first line):
  - "AUTO-ROLLBACK executed" / "AUTO-PAUSE executed" / "AUTO-ROLLBACK suppressed" / "SLO monitor stale"
- Rollout scope: service/route, model_version, baseline_version (if present), region, customer_tier
- Policy context: policy_name + policy_version
- SLO snapshot summary (safe + compact):
  - which signals tripped (p95/p99 latency, 5xx)
  - burn-rate windows used (fast/slow) and whether both confirmed
  - sample size summary + staleness timestamp if relevant
- Links:
  - Console rollout URL (rollout_id)
  - Grafana dashboard deep link (rollout_id filter)
  - Monitor definition link (if available)
- Correlation: event_id + trace/correlation_id

Privacy / compliance
- Do not include customer identifiers (org name, request ids, API keys) in Slack messages.
- OK to include customer_tier and region.

Noise controls (must-have)
- Dedupe: if the same action for the same rollout_id + step_id is seen within 10 minutes, suppress duplicates.
- Rate limit: max 3 messages / 5 minutes per rollout_id; additional events should be aggregated into a single summary message "(N similar events suppressed; see Console)".
- Threading: use one Slack thread per rollout_id in #eng-oncall so follow-on events (e.g., repeated suppressions) attach to the initial executed action.
- Backoff on Slack API errors; do not retry indefinitely (avoid backlog storms).

Acceptance criteria
- In staging, synthetic SLO breach triggers a single Slack message within 60s.
- Repeated evaluation ticks during the same breach do not spam Slack (verified by dedupe/rate limit).
- Executed rollback posts to #eng-oncall and service channel (if configured).
- Suppression events do not post to #eng-oncall.
- Missing/stale monitor events post to #eng-sre with actionable next step ("monitor missing/stale; rollout will fail-open per policy") and correct links.
- Unit tests cover:
  - dedupe keying
  - per-rollout rate limiting
  - thread id caching
  - formatting for each event type

Implementation notes
- incident-bot should maintain a small in-memory + Redis cache (if available) keyed by rollout_id: {thread_ts, last_sent_at, dedupe_window_map}.
- Prefer deterministic dedupe key: sha256(rollout_id + step_id + action + policy_version + fast_window + slow_window).
- Ensure we tag messages with environment (staging/prod) and region to avoid confusion during game days.

References
- GitHub: incident-bot PR: sources/github/incident-bot/pr-477-post-auto-rollback-events-to-slack.json
- Audit events: sources/github/redwood/pr-28435-add-rollout-audit-events-to-event-stream.json
- Alerting + dashboard routing discussion: sources/slack/eng-oncall/1770311020.json, sources/slack/eng-sre/1770619940.json

- Suppressed + missing/stale monitor: post to #eng-sre (notify) (not #eng-oncall) unless suppression reason is "KNOWN_INCIDENT" (then do nothing to avoid noise during active incident).

2) Message format (single message per event)
Include:
- Action + short reason (first line):
  - "AUTO-ROLLBACK executed" / "AUTO-PAUSE executed" / "AUTO-ROLLBACK suppressed" / "SLO monitor stale"
- Rollout scope: service/route, model_version, baseline_version (if present), region, customer_tier
- Policy context: policy_name + policy_version
- SLO snapshot summary (safe + compact):
  - which signals tripped (p95/p99 latency, 5xx)
  - burn-rate windows used (fast/slow) and whether both confirmed
  - sample size summary + staleness timestamp if relevant
- Links:
  - Console rollout URL (rollout_id)
  - Grafana dashboard deep link (rollout_id filter)
  - Monitor definition link (if available)
- Correlation: event_id + trace/correlation_id

Privacy / compliance
- Do not include customer identifiers (org name, request ids, API keys) in Slack messages.
- OK to include customer_tier and region.

Noise controls (must-have)
- Dedupe: if the same action for the same rollout_id + step_id is seen within 10 minutes, suppress duplicates.
- Rate limit: max 3 messages / 5 minutes per rollout_id; additional events should be aggregated into a single summary message "(N similar events suppressed; see Console)".
- Threading: use one Slack thread per rollout_id in #eng-oncall so follow-on events (e.g., repeated suppressions) attach to the initial executed action.
- Backoff on Slack API errors; do not retry indefinitely (avoid backlog storms).

Acceptance criteria
- In staging, synthetic SLO breach triggers a single Slack message within 60s.
- Repeated evaluation ticks during the same breach do not spam Slack (verified by dedupe/rate limit).
- Executed rollback posts to #eng-oncall and service channel (if configured).
- Suppression events do not post to #eng-oncall.
- Missing/stale monitor events post to #eng-sre with actionable next step ("monitor missing/stale; rollout will fail-open per policy") and correct links.
- Unit tests cover:
  - dedupe keying
  - per-rollout rate limiting
  - thread id caching
  - formatting for each event type

Implementation notes
- incident-bot should maintain a small in-memory + Redis cache (if available) keyed by rollout_id: {thread_ts, last_sent_at, dedupe_window_map}.
- Prefer deterministic dedupe key: sha256(rollout_id + step_id + action + policy_version + fast_window + slow_window).
- Ensure we tag messages with environment (staging/prod) and region to avoid confusion during game days.

References
- GitHub: incident-bot PR: sources/github/incident-bot/pr-477-post-auto-rollback-events-to-slack.json
- Audit events: sources/github/redwood/pr-28435-add-rollout-audit-events-to-event-stream.json
- Alerting + dashboard routing discussion: sources/slack/eng-oncall/1770311020.json, sources/slack/eng-sre/1770619940.json

title: Plan + execute game day for SLO-triggered auto-rollback (pause/rollback/suppress) and capture follow-ups
preview:
## Goal
Run an incident-style game day to validate end-to-end behavior for SLO-driven auto-mitigation during rollouts:
- Detect latency/error SLO breach scoped to rollout (route/model version/region/tier)
- Apply configured policy action (pause vs rollback vs suppress)
- Produce auditable decision trail + Console visibility
- Avoid alert noise/paging storms
- Validate staleness behavior and “known incident” suppression

This ticket owns: planning, execution, and capturing concrete follow-ups (owners + tracking).

## References
- Game day plan (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/site-reliability-engineering/auto-rollback-game-day-plan.json
- Runbook draft (Confluence): sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/auto-rollback-on-slo-breach-runbook.json
- Postmortem motivating gaps: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/postmortems/2026-02-17-rollout-latency-regression-auto-rollback-gaps.json
- Metrics naming standard: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/rollouts-slo-metrics-naming-and-labeling.json

## Participants / roles
- Incident commander: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Observability lead: Aisha Bello
- Rollout controller: Jada Williams
- Console / audit trail: Felix Schneider
- Policy engine: Jordan Lee
- Release/QA support: Leila Farouk

## Preconditions (must be true before we run)
1) Feature flag enabled in staging for one internal service/route (canary rollout type).
2) SLO monitors exist for the scoped dimensions (at minimum: p95 latency + 5xx error rate).
3) Incident-bot Slack notifications enabled in non-paging mode (notify-only channel).
4) Dashboards accessible to oncall group.
5) Runbook draft is up-to-date with expected controller behavior.

## Test environment
- Env: staging
- Candidate service/route: redwood-api /v1/chat.completions (internal canary route)
- Regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1
- Tiers: internal/self-serve
- Rollout type(s): canary traffic split + step-based rollout

## Scenarios executed
### Scenario A — Fast burn latency regression during canary
Inject tail latency increase for canary model version only (simulate runtime regression):
- Expected: controller pauses rollout at current step (MVP) OR rolls back if policy action=rollback
- Expected: audit event emitted with monitor ids + burn rate + window state + sample sizes
- Expected: Slack notification (notify-only) + Grafana dashboard shows “auto decision”

### Scenario B — 5xx burst (error rate SLO breach)
Inject elevated 5xx (synthetic) for canary target only:
- Expected: immediate pause/rollback based on policy template (enterprise defaults more aggressive)
- Expected: no oscillation (debounce/holdoff respected)

### Scenario C — Monitor staleness / missing data
Simulate telemetry lag / monitor query returning stale timestamp:
- Expected: controller does NOT rollback based on missing/stale signals
- Expected: decision outcome recorded as “suppressed: monitor_stale” or “no_action: monitor_missing”
- Expected: alert fires to notify channel for “monitor stale/missing for active rollout” (non-paging)

### Scenario D — Known incident suppression window
Mark an active incident suppression window (simulate ongoing infra incident):
- Expected: SLO breach detected but action suppressed with reason “known_incident_override”
- Expected: audit event includes suppression metadata + who/what set suppression (admin override)

### Scenario E — Rollback executor safety / idempotency under retries
Force a partial failure during rollback execution (e.g., transient control plane timeout) and retry:
- Expected: rollback converges safely; idempotent execution; no double-traffic-shift

## Execution notes (2026-02-25)
- 10:00–10:10 PT: kickoff, checklist confirmed, dashboards open.
- 10:10–10:25 PT: Scenario A (latency) executed in us-west-2.
- 10:25–10:40 PT: Scenario B (5xx) executed in us-west-2.
- 10:40–10:55 PT: Scenario C (stale) executed by disabling telemetry feed for scoped labels.
- 10:55–11:15 PT: Scenario D (suppression) executed; verified audit trail + Console view.
- 11:15–11:35 PT: Scenario E (idempotency) executed; forced retry path.

## Results summary
- ✅ Auto-pause triggered correctly for fast-burn latency and 5xx scenarios once min-sample threshold reached.
- ✅ Audit events were emitted and visible via Console API for the rollout id (filter + pagination worked).
- ✅ Slack notification posted once per decision (no storm) and linked to dashboard + rollout id.
- ⚠️ Monitor staleness handling behaved inconsistently between latency and error-rate monitors (see Findings #1).
- ⚠️ Debounce/holdoff prevented oscillation, but delayed rollback longer than expected in Scenario B under low QPS (see Findings #2).
- ⚠️ One UI panel state did not refresh automatically after rollback; required manual reload (see Findings #5).

## Findings / gaps
1) **Staleness classification mismatch (latency vs error monitors).**
   - Observed: latency burn-rate endpoint returned `stale=true` with timestamp > 6m old; controller suppressed as expected.
   - Observed: error-rate endpoint returned `unknown` state with no staleness bit; controller treated as “no breach” (fail-open) and continued rollout.
   - Risk: silent miss on stale error-rate signals.
   - Owner: Aisha / Jordan

2) **Min-sample gating interacts badly with fast-burn expectations at low QPS.**
   - For internal/self-serve routes with low baseline traffic in staging, reaching min sample took ~7–9 minutes.
   - Policy template expects 5m fast burn; in practice we triggered at ~9m.
   - Need: staging override, or allow min-sample to be expressed as both absolute and % of expected RPS.
   - Owner: Sean / Jordan

3) **Suppression audit trail missing “suppression scope resolution.”**
   - Audit event includes suppression reason + policy version, but not the computed scope (e.g., “service default suppression applied” vs “rollout override suppression applied”).
   - Makes incident review harder.
   - Owner: Felix

4) **Alert routing: monitor missing/stale should page only when in production and rollout step > X%.**
   - Current alert rule in staging is notify-only (OK), but production rule proposal would page on any staleness.
   - Recommendation: page only if rollout is active + traffic shifted >= 25% + monitor missing/stale persists > 10m.
   - Owner: Aisha

5) **Console: audit timeline refresh bug after action executes.**
   - Timeline did not show the latest event until page refresh; API had the event.
   - Likely UI subscription/cache invalidation issue.
   - Owner: Felix / Hannah

6) **Rollback executor retries were safe, but the “rate-limited shift” took longer than expected.**
   - Step-down strategy respected limits (good), but total time to revert to baseline at 50% canary was ~4m.
   - For enterprise tier we may need a faster “emergency revert” mode with separate guardrails.
   - Owner: Jada / Ethan

## Follow-ups (created / to be created)
- [ ] Update monitor adapter to normalize staleness/unknown states across SLO types; ensure controller treats unknown as suppress + alert (ties to ENG-4826).
- [ ] Revisit defaults for min-sample gating; add staging template and document expected behavior (ties to ENG-4861 + PM-618).
- [ ] Extend audit schema to include suppression scope resolution + computed scope fields (ties to ADR-0128 + ENG-4831/ENG-4833).
- [ ] Tune alert rules for stale/missing monitors to avoid paging storms (ties to ENG-4840).
- [ ] Fix Console audit timeline refresh (ties to ENG-4833).
- [ ] Evaluate rollback executor “emergency revert” mode proposal for high-tier incidents (ties to ENG-4829).

## Exit criteria (met)
- All scenarios executed at least once in staging.
- Findings documented with owners and linked to existing workstreams.
- Runbook updated with observed behavior + gaps (Rafael to PR Confluence edits).

title: Shadow traffic load testing + overhead benchmarks (validate no impact to user-path SLOs)
preview:
Goal
Validate that enabling shadow traffic does not materially impact the user (control) request path SLOs and that all guardrails behave as designed under load and failure modes.

This ticket covers:
- load generation + benchmark harness
- p50/p95/p99 latency overhead on the control path (chat/generate + streaming)
- CPU overhead (router / data plane) and memory growth
- backpressure/circuit breaker behavior when shadow path is slow or down
- telemetry and scoring pipeline saturation safety checks (shadow path must degrade without impacting user path)

Why
Shadowing introduces extra work in the router (fingerprinting/bucketing, enqueue/mirror, metadata, partial capture for streaming). We must prove the user path remains within SLO and overhead budgets, and document a go/no-go gate for beta enablement.

Success criteria / acceptance
1) Control-path latency overhead with shadowing enabled:
- p50: <= +1.0 ms absolute
- p95: <= +3.0 ms absolute
- p99: <= +8.0 ms absolute
Measured at router boundary (request received -> forwarded to primary target) and end-to-end API latency (client observed), for both non-streaming and streaming.

2) CPU overhead:
- Router CPU: <= +5% at steady-state vs baseline for same QPS profile
- No unbounded goroutine/thread growth; no sustained GC pressure increase >20% vs baseline (Go data plane pods)

3) Fail-open / guardrails:
- If shadow target p99 > timeout threshold OR error rate > circuit-breaker threshold, shadowing must auto-disable per-route without increasing control-path errors.
- If shadow event ingestion queue is saturated, shadow event emission should sample/drop with explicit counters, and must not block the control path.

4) Quotas:
- Shadow requests must not count against end-user request quotas (only against a dedicated internal “shadow budget”), and must not trip customer-facing rate limiters.

Bench plan
Workloads (representative of beta):
A) Chat/completions (non-stream):
- prompt tokens: p50 ~250, p95 ~900
- output tokens: 128 (fixed) and 512 (burst)
- concurrency: 50/200/500
- QPS profiles: steady and bursty (5x for 60s)

B) Streaming chat:
- same prompt distributions
- stream chunk size default
- ensure shadow capture semantics match ENG-5414 (capture final assembled output, allow early stop on timeout)

Shadow configs tested:
- sampling: 0% (baseline), 1%, 5%, 10%
- targets: 1 shadow variant and 2 shadow variants
- shadow timeouts: 150ms and 300ms router-side (shadow path only)

Failure modes tested:
- shadow target 5xx at 20% and 80%
- shadow target latency injected (p99 +800ms)
- ingestion sink backpressure (simulate Kafka/queue lag)
- scoring queue backlog (worker down)

Test environment
- Dedicated perf cluster (us-east-1), isolated from prod
- Router build: runtime-1.22-rc3 + shadow traffic patches (ENG-5402, ENG-5415)
- Primary model target: “rw-llama3.1-70b” (dedicated pool)
- Shadow target: “rw-llama3.1-70b-quant” (separate pool) and “rw-llama3.1-8b” for 2-target scenarios
- Load tool: perf-canary suite + k6 wrapper

Artifacts / code
- Bench suite PR: sources/github/perf-canary/pr-177-shadow-traffic-overhead-benchmark-suite.json
- Router mirroring: sources/github/redwood/pr-24833-routing-mirror-sampled-requests-fail-open.json
- Circuit breaker/backpressure: sources/github/redwood/pr-24895-shadow-pipeline-backpressure-and-circuit-breaker.json
- Streaming shadow semantics: sources/github/redwood/pr-24904-shadowing-support-for-streaming-requests.json

Results summary (2026-02-18 run)
Control-path latency overhead (router boundary)
Non-streaming (primary only, shadow enabled):
- 1% sampling (1 target): p50 +0.3ms, p95 +0.9ms, p99 +2.1ms
- 5% sampling (1 target): p50 +0.6ms, p95 +1.8ms, p99 +4.7ms
- 10% sampling (1 target): p50 +0.9ms, p95 +2.6ms, p99 +7.4ms
- 10% sampling (2 targets): p50 +1.2ms, p95 +3.5ms, p99 +9.8ms (p99 slightly above target)

Streaming (control stream to client, shadow capture enabled):
- 5% sampling (1 target): p50 +0.7ms, p95 +2.1ms, p99 +6.2ms
- 10% sampling (1 target): p50 +1.1ms, p95 +3.0ms, p99 +8.6ms (borderline p99)

CPU / memory
- Router CPU at 5% sampling (1 target): +3.2% mean; +4.8% at burst peak; within budget
- Router CPU at 10% sampling (2 targets): +6.9% mean; exceeded budget; correlated with additional serialization + event emission
- Memory: stable; no leak observed over 2h soak; minor increase (~+90MB per pod) due to buffering for streaming shadow capture (expected)

Guardrails / fail-open
- Shadow target latency injection: circuit breaker tripped within ~25s; shadowing disabled for route; control-path latency returned to baseline immediately (no elevated error rate)
- Shadow target 80% 5xx: breaker tripped; control path unaffected; shadow error counter incremented as expected
- Ingestion backpressure: event emission dropped at ~40% during induced lag; control path unaffected; drops visible via new counters (needs dashboard wiring)
- Scoring queue backlog: no effect on control path (as expected; async)

Conclusion / go-no-go
- GO for beta defaults at: max 5% sampling, max 1 shadow target for Dedicated tenants unless explicitly approved.
- For 10% sampling and/or 2 shadow targets, p99 and CPU budgets are at risk depending on workload; requires capacity review + dedicated perf validation per tenant.

Follow-ups / action items
1) Tighten default validation: disallow 2 shadow targets for streaming routes at >5% sampling unless feature flag “shadow_multi_target_streaming” enabled. (Owner: Benji Okafor)
2) Reduce overhead for 2-target mode: avoid double JSON serialization of request body; reuse canonical bytes / use zero-copy buffer where possible. (Owner: Mina Haddad)
3) Add dashboards for new counters: shadow_event_drop_rate, breaker_open, shadow_mirror_timeout. (Owner: Allison Grant)
4) Document “safe sampling” guidance and beta runbook updates (include these measured numbers). (Owner: Elliot Price + Sean Gallagher)

Notes
- Measurements are relative to baseline build without shadow policy parsing/bucketing logic. All tests ran with the same primary target GPU pool.
- We observed that streaming shadow capture cost is driven by buffering of assembled output; using partial capture (first N tokens) reduces memory but reduces scoring quality. Kept full capture for v1; use strict timeouts + sampling defaults to manage cost.

- 1% sampling (1 target): p50 +0.3ms, p95 +0.9ms, p99 +2.1ms
- 5% sampling (1 target): p50 +0.6ms, p95 +1.8ms, p99 +4.7ms
- 10% sampling (1 target): p50 +0.9ms, p95 +2.6ms, p99 +7.4ms
- 10% sampling (2 targets): p50 +1.2ms, p95 +3.5ms, p99 +9.8ms (p99 slightly above target)

Streaming (control stream to client, shadow capture enabled):
- 5% sampling (1 target): p50 +0.7ms, p95 +2.1ms, p99 +6.2ms
- 10% sampling (1 target): p50 +1.1ms, p95 +3.0ms, p99 +8.6ms (borderline p99)

CPU / memory
- Router CPU at 5% sampling (1 target): +3.2% mean; +4.8% at burst peak; within budget
- Router CPU at 10% sampling (2 targets): +6.9% mean; exceeded budget; correlated with additional serialization + event emission
- Memory: stable; no leak observed over 2h soak; minor increase (~+90MB per pod) due to buffering for streaming shadow capture (expected)

Guardrails / fail-open
- Shadow target latency injection: circuit breaker tripped within ~25s; shadowing disabled for route; control-path latency returned to baseline immediately (no elevated error rate)
- Shadow target 80% 5xx: breaker tripped; control path unaffected; shadow error counter incremented as expected
- Ingestion backpressure: event emission dropped at ~40% during induced lag; control path unaffected; drops visible via new counters (needs dashboard wiring)
- Scoring queue backlog: no effect on control path (as expected; async)

Conclusion / go-no-go
- GO for beta defaults at: max 5% sampling, max 1 shadow target for Dedicated tenants unless explicitly approved.
- For 10% sampling and/or 2 shadow targets, p99 and CPU budgets are at risk depending on workload; requires capacity review + dedicated perf validation per tenant.

Follow-ups / action items
1) Tighten default validation: disallow 2 shadow targets for streaming routes at >5% sampling unless feature flag “shadow_multi_target_streaming” enabled. (Owner: Benji Okafor)
2) Reduce overhead for 2-target mode: avoid double JSON serialization of request body; reuse canonical bytes / use zero-copy buffer where possible. (Owner: Mina Haddad)
3) Add dashboards for new counters: shadow_event_drop_rate, breaker_open, shadow_mirror_timeout. (Owner: Allison Grant)
4) Document “safe sampling” guidance and beta runbook updates (include these measured numbers). (Owner: Elliot Price + Sean Gallagher)

Notes
- Measurements are relative to baseline build without shadow policy parsing/bucketing logic. All tests ran with the same primary target GPU pool.
- We observed that streaming shadow capture cost is driven by buffering of assembled output; using partial capture (first N tokens) reduces memory but reduces scoring quality. Kept full capture for v1; use strict timeouts + sampling defaults to manage cost.

title: Add canonical dashboard links to Support runbooks + publish Support-safe Quick View dashboards
preview:
## Context
Support escalations (SUP tickets + Slack bridges) routinely stall on the same questions:
- “Is this a region issue vs customer-specific?”
- “Are we seeing elevated 5xx/429s?”
- “Is streaming unhealthy (disconnects/resets)?”

SRE/on-call has dashboards, but links aren’t consistently discoverable from runbooks, and many views aren’t appropriate for Support to access directly (too much internal detail, noisy, or requires elevated permissions). This ticket creates:
1) canonical dashboard links referenced from runbooks/templates, and
2) a Support-safe “Quick View” set of dashboards (aggregate only) that Support can use during triage and while writing customer updates.

This work is part of the Feb 2026 escalation playbook refresh and known-issues sprint, where we’re standardizing required context for pages and reducing time-to-triage.

## Goals
- Provide a single, canonical set of dashboard links for:
  - API latency (p50/p95/p99) by region + route
  - API error rate (4xx/5xx) by region + route
  - Streaming health (disconnect/reset/truncation proxies)
- Make those dashboards accessible to Support via a Support-safe role (read-only, aggregate metrics).
- Reduce back-and-forth on “can you check X dashboard” by embedding the links in runbooks and escalation templates.

## Non-goals
- Building new metrics (tracked separately; see ENG-4821 and ENG-4824).
- Giving Support access to raw logs/traces.
- Redesigning the full on-call runbook IA; this ticket is limited to link hygiene + a minimal Quick View set.

## Proposed approach
### A) Canonical dashboards + stable links
Create stable shortlinks for the canonical dashboards (so runbooks don’t rot when dashboards get duplicated):
- Latency: “API Latency (Support Quick View)”
- Errors: “API Errors (Support Quick View)”
- Streaming: “Streaming Health (Support Quick View)”

Implementation notes:
- Use a dedicated Grafana folder: “Support Quick View” (read-only for Support group).
- For each dashboard:
  - Default time range: last 30m
  - Global variables: region, route (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank), tier (hosted/dedicated/private where applicable)
  - Panels limited to aggregate metrics only (no request payloads, no customer identifiers)

### B) Runbook updates (minimum viable)
Update the primary Support escalation/runbook surfaces to include a “Dashboards” section with the canonical links:
- “Support escalation template” (the pinned bridge template used in #support / bridge channels)
- “Streaming disconnects” runbook section (links for streaming health + latency/errors)
- “Regional degradation” runbook section (links for latency/errors)

This ticket owns adding the links and verifying access; the Confluence content updates are coordinated with Support/Docs but the engineering deliverable is the canonical dashboards + link set.

## Canonical links (internal)
Note: these are internal-only and intended for Redwood Support + on-call. Do not share with customers.
- API Latency (Support Quick View): https://grafana.internal.redwood.ai/d/sup-qv-latency
- API Errors (Support Quick View): https://grafana.internal.redwood.ai/d/sup-qv-errors
- Streaming Health (Support Quick View): https://grafana.internal.redwood.ai/d/sup-qv-streaming

## Support-safe constraints
- No customer identifiers (account IDs, org names, IPs).
- No raw request/response logs.
- No per-customer slices unless explicitly approved and implemented as an internal-only drilldown with restricted access.
- Any export/download features remain disabled for Support role if possible (Grafana setting/policy dependent).

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] “Support Quick View” folder exists with three dashboards (latency, errors, streaming).
- [x] Support role/group can view dashboards without escalations to SRE.
- [x] Links are stable (shortlinks) and referenced from the Support escalation template/runbook surfaces.
- [x] On-call reviewers confirm the dashboards answer the top triage questions in <5 minutes.

## Validation plan
- Dry run with Support leadership and on-call:
  - Use 2–3 recent tickets as scenarios (streaming resets, 429 confusion, regional timeouts).
  - Confirm Support can:
    1) identify whether issue is broad vs isolated,
    2) capture a screenshot + summary for Slack bridge,
    3) include correct, non-overpromising language in customer updates.

## Rollout
- Announce in #support and #eng-oncall with the three canonical links and intended usage.
- Add to pinned bridge template via INT-332/INT-333 follow-ups (Support automation + Jira prompt).

## Stakeholders
- Support Ops: Dev Patel
- SRE: Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes
- Observability advisor: Allison Grant
- Docs/Program: Elliot Price
- Security guidance (policy/least privilege): Naomi Feldman

## Related work
- ENG-4821 Add Support signal to routing/fallback metrics
- ENG-4822 Improve error message for streaming disconnect
- ENG-4824 Add audit log lag metric + alert
- ENG-4830 Incident bot Jira linking for SUP tickets

## Notes / open questions (resolved)
- Q: Should Support get access to the full on-call dashboards?
  - Decision: No. Maintain a minimal “Quick View” folder that is explicitly safe and fast.
- Q: Do we need a per-model breakdown?
  - Decision: Provide route + region by default; model breakdown optional as a variable only if cardinality is safe and the panel remains readable.

title: Dedicated autoscaling stability guardrails (cooldowns + min capacity enforcement) to prevent oscillation
preview:
## Context
Dedicated GPU pools have shown autoscaling oscillation (“churn”) under spiky token-rate workloads and when queue depth signals flap. This manifests as latency spikes (p95/p99) and occasional elevated error rates when scale-down happens too aggressively and warm-up costs dominate.

This is a top escalation driver for Dedicated customers and is explicitly referenced in Support’s Known Issues Sprint.

**Primary escalation:** SUP-1844 (Dedicated autoscaling oscillation causing latency)

## Problem statement
Current autoscaling policies can:
- scale down quickly after a short dip in load, then scale back up within minutes (repeated cycles)
- allow effective capacity to drop below a customer’s “safe minimum” during transient dips
- cause repeated cold starts / model warm-up that increase tail latency and amplify paging noise

## Goals
1) Add guardrails to reduce oscillation without materially harming steady-state cost.
2) Ensure a configurable minimum capacity floor is enforced (per Dedicated pool / per model route as applicable).
3) Make behavior observable so Support/SRE can confirm if guardrails are active during an escalation.

## Non-goals
- Replacing the autoscaler architecture.
- Reworking customer-specific scaling strategies beyond a small set of safe defaults.
- Introducing tier routing changes (separate workstream).

## Proposed guardrails (v1)
### A) Scale-down cooldown (hard guardrail)
- Introduce a minimum time window after any scale-up event during which scale-down is disallowed.
- Default: **15 minutes** (subject to SRE review; customers with very bursty traffic may need 20–30m).
- Rationale: most oscillation events observed in SUP-1844 occur within a 5–12m window.

### B) Minimum capacity enforcement (soft + hard)
- Enforce `min_replicas` at the control plane level even if the autoscaler would otherwise reduce below it.
- Add a “floor reason” annotation to the scaling decision record (e.g., `min_capacity_floor_enforced`).
- Ensure UI/config validation prevents `min_replicas` > `max_replicas` and flags overly small max values.

### C) Scale-down step limits
- Limit per-decision scale-down delta (e.g., max 20% down per 5m decision window) to avoid large drops.
- This is optional for v1 if implementation risk is high; keep behind a feature flag.

### D) Signal smoothing
- Apply simple EMA smoothing to queue depth / utilization signal used by autoscaler.
- Also optional for v1; prefer cooldown + floor first.

## Implementation notes
- Owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE), with support from Platform control plane (Ethan Park) for config + validation.
- Feature flag: `dedicated_autoscaling_guardrails_v1` (default off → staged rollout).
- Store scaling decisions + reasons in the existing autoscaling events table (or equivalent) to support dashboarding.

## Observability requirements
Dependencies:
- **ENG-4823**: add canonical dashboard links; we should ensure Dedicated autoscaling dashboard includes:
  - replicas over time
  - scale events (up/down)
  - cooldown active indicator
  - min-capacity floor enforcement count
  - latency overlay (p95/p99)
- **ENG-4821**: support-facing signals for routing/fallback; not strictly required for this change but helpful for incident triage.

Minimum instrumentation to add in this ticket:
- counter: `dedicated.autoscaler.scale_down_blocked_total{reason=cooldown}`
- counter: `dedicated.autoscaler.floor_enforced_total`
- gauge: `dedicated.autoscaler.cooldown_active` (or derived from events)

## Rollout plan
1) Implement guardrails behind flag; verify in staging with load replay.
2) Enable on 1 internal Dedicated pool (dogfood) for 48 hours.
3) Enable for 1–2 low-risk Dedicated customers who have experienced oscillation (with CS alignment).
4) Expand rollout to all Dedicated pools; update Support KB with final recommended defaults.

## Acceptance criteria
- Under synthetic spiky load test, observed scale cycles (up→down→up within 30m) reduced by **>= 70%**.
- No increase in steady-state p95 latency > **5%** for stable workloads.
- `min_replicas` is never violated (validated by event audit + periodic assertion).
- Support/SRE can identify “cooldown preventing scale-down” from dashboards/event annotations.

## Risks / trade-offs
- Cooldowns may increase cost during legitimate load drops (intended; we should keep defaults conservative and configurable).
- Min floor enforcement can mask misconfiguration (too-high min). Add config validation and warnings.
- If signal smoothing is added, ensure it doesn’t delay necessary scale-ups (prefer to only smooth for scale-down decisions).

## Support/CS coordination
- Ensure Support playbook reflects correct knob names and safe ranges; do not suggest bypassing on-call processes.
- Update known-issue article with: how to confirm oscillation, what settings we changed, and when to escalate.

## Notes from SRE review (02/12)
- Sean requested we align default cooldown with paging thresholds (avoid repeated paging during churn).
- Rafael to confirm whether cooldown should be per-pool or per-model-route (preference: per-pool first for simplicity).

## Current status / next steps
- [In progress] Spec + defaults review with SRE/Platform (02/17)
- [Next] Implement cooldown + min floor enforcement (target PR by 02/19)
- [Next] Add event reason annotations + counters
- [Next] Stage rollout + dogfood

## Links
- SUP escalation: SUP-1844
- Known-issues KB: workaround-dedicated-autoscaling-oscillation
- SLA/SLO mapping reference: support-sla-vs-engineering-slo-mapping

title: Add metrics + dashboard + alerts for offline bundle signing pipeline health
preview:
Context
Offline bundle signing is now a release gating requirement for Redwood Private. We have had intermittent CI issues in the signing stage (timeouts/HSM emulator instability in staging; occasional retries masking real regressions). We also need better early warning for key-rotation readiness (e.g., impending cert expiry / trust-store drift) so air-gapped customers are not surprised.

Goal
Provide end-to-end visibility into the signing pipeline so Release Eng + SRE can answer:
- Is signing healthy right now?
- Are signed bundles being produced within expected latency?
- What are the top failure causes (HSM/KMS, canonicalization, upload/publish, verification tests)?
- Are we at risk for a key rotation event (expiring signing cert, trust store not updated, installers not compatible)?

Non-goals
- Re-architect the signing pipeline (tracked elsewhere).
- Build customer-facing telemetry (Private is offline; exporter integrations handled separately).

Success criteria (definition of done)
1) Metrics emitted for signing pipeline runs + stage-level outcomes in prod CI.
2) A Grafana dashboard exists with an SRE-friendly overview + drill-down.
3) Alert rules exist for:
   - sustained signing failures
   - p95 signing duration regression
   - missing signed bundle artifact for a release candidate
   - key-rotation readiness thresholds (e.g., cert expiry < 30/14/7 days)
4) Runbook page linked from alerts with concrete triage steps + owners.
5) Release gating checklist updated to reference dashboard/alerts as required pre-cut check.

Scope / instrumentation plan
A) CI pipeline instrumentation (Release pipeline)
- Source: CI job metadata (GitHub Actions + internal build orchestrator events).
- Emit as metrics to our standard pipeline metrics sink (Prometheus remote-write via build-metrics sidecar).
- Metric names (proposal; align with existing naming conventions):
  - redwood_release_signing_run_total{result="success|failure|canceled", branch, workflow, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_stage_total{stage, result, workflow, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_duration_seconds_bucket{workflow, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_stage_duration_seconds_bucket{stage, workflow, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_artifact_publish_total{result, artifact_type="bundle|manifest|signature|sbom", environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_verification_tests_total{result, suite="tamper|replay|downgrade|time_skew", environment}
- Required labels: workflow, environment (staging/prod), release_channel (rc/ga), git_ref.
- Avoid high-cardinality labels (no per-commit SHA label; use git_ref normalized to release tag for RC/GA runs).

B) Key rotation readiness metrics
- Source: signing key metadata in KMS/HSM and release trust-store repository state.
- Emit:
  - redwood_release_signing_key_not_after_timestamp{key_id, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_key_not_before_timestamp{key_id, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_trust_store_keys_total{environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_trust_store_age_seconds{environment} (time since last trust store update merged)
  - redwood_release_signing_installer_compat_matrix_status{status="ok|warning|blocked", environment}
- Notes:
  - The timestamp metrics should be collected by a scheduled job (e.g., daily) with minimal privileges (read-only key metadata).
  - In prod we may not be able to read full cert material; just expiration and key id/alias.

C) Grafana dashboard
Panels (MVP):
1) Overview row
- Signing success rate (last 24h / 7d)
- Total signing runs by environment/channel
- p50/p95 signing duration
- Current open incidents/alerts (link to alert list)
2) Failure diagnostics
- Failures by stage (stacked)
- Top error classes (parse from structured step exit reasons if available; otherwise stage + exit code mapping)
- Verification test failures trend
3) Artifact publishing
- Published bundles count by release tag
- Missing artifact detector (see alert section)
4) Key readiness
- Days until cert expiry (min across active keys)
- Trust store keys count + last updated age
- Installer compatibility status (manual input ok for MVP; automated later)

D) Alerting (Alertmanager)
Alert rules (initial thresholds; tune after 2 weeks):
1) Signing failures
- Condition: redwood_release_signing_run_total{result="failure", environment="prod"} / total > 0.10 for 30m
- Severity: page (if release window), otherwise ticket
- Annotation: link to dashboard + runbook
2) Signing duration regression
- Condition: p95(redwood_release_signing_duration_seconds) > baseline * 1.5 for 6h OR absolute > 25m for prod
- Severity: ticket (page if blocks release train)
3) Missing signed artifacts for RC/GA tag
- Condition: signing succeeded but publish_total missing for required artifact types within 20m
- Severity: page during release cut
4) Key expiry warnings
- Condition: (redwood_release_signing_key_not_after_timestamp - now) < 30d (warning), < 14d (ticket), < 7d (page)
- Severity mapping as above
5) Trust store staleness
- Condition: trust_store_age_seconds > 30d (ticket) or > 45d (page)

Owners / on-call routing

Context
Offline bundle signing is now a release gating requirement for Redwood Private. We have had intermittent CI issues in the signing stage (timeouts/HSM emulator instability in staging; occasional retries masking real regressions). We also need better early warning for key-rotation readiness (e.g., impending cert expiry / trust-store drift) so air-gapped customers are not surprised.

Goal
Provide end-to-end visibility into the signing pipeline so Release Eng + SRE can answer:
- Is signing healthy right now?
- Are signed bundles being produced within expected latency?
- What are the top failure causes (HSM/KMS, canonicalization, upload/publish, verification tests)?
- Are we at risk for a key rotation event (expiring signing cert, trust store not updated, installers not compatible)?

Non-goals
- Re-architect the signing pipeline (tracked elsewhere).
- Build customer-facing telemetry (Private is offline; exporter integrations handled separately).

Success criteria (definition of done)
1) Metrics emitted for signing pipeline runs + stage-level outcomes in prod CI.
2) A Grafana dashboard exists with an SRE-friendly overview + drill-down.
3) Alert rules exist for:
   - sustained signing failures
   - p95 signing duration regression
   - missing signed bundle artifact for a release candidate
   - key-rotation readiness thresholds (e.g., cert expiry < 30/14/7 days)
4) Runbook page linked from alerts with concrete triage steps + owners.
5) Release gating checklist updated to reference dashboard/alerts as required pre-cut check.

Scope / instrumentation plan
A) CI pipeline instrumentation (Release pipeline)
- Source: CI job metadata (GitHub Actions + internal build orchestrator events).
- Emit as metrics to our standard pipeline metrics sink (Prometheus remote-write via build-metrics sidecar).
- Metric names (proposal; align with existing naming conventions):
  - redwood_release_signing_run_total{result="success|failure|canceled", branch, workflow, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_stage_total{stage, result, workflow, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_duration_seconds_bucket{workflow, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_stage_duration_seconds_bucket{stage, workflow, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_artifact_publish_total{result, artifact_type="bundle|manifest|signature|sbom", environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_verification_tests_total{result, suite="tamper|replay|downgrade|time_skew", environment}
- Required labels: workflow, environment (staging/prod), release_channel (rc/ga), git_ref.
- Avoid high-cardinality labels (no per-commit SHA label; use git_ref normalized to release tag for RC/GA runs).

B) Key rotation readiness metrics
- Source: signing key metadata in KMS/HSM and release trust-store repository state.
- Emit:
  - redwood_release_signing_key_not_after_timestamp{key_id, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_key_not_before_timestamp{key_id, environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_trust_store_keys_total{environment}
  - redwood_release_signing_trust_store_age_seconds{environment} (time since last trust store update merged)
  - redwood_release_signing_installer_compat_matrix_status{status="ok|warning|blocked", environment}
- Notes:
  - The timestamp metrics should be collected by a scheduled job (e.g., daily) with minimal privileges (read-only key metadata).
  - In prod we may not be able to read full cert material; just expiration and key id/alias.

C) Grafana dashboard
Panels (MVP):
1) Overview row
- Signing success rate (last 24h / 7d)
- Total signing runs by environment/channel
- p50/p95 signing duration
- Current open incidents/alerts (link to alert list)
2) Failure diagnostics
- Failures by stage (stacked)
- Top error classes (parse from structured step exit reasons if available; otherwise stage + exit code mapping)
- Verification test failures trend
3) Artifact publishing
- Published bundles count by release tag
- Missing artifact detector (see alert section)
4) Key readiness
- Days until cert expiry (min across active keys)
- Trust store keys count + last updated age
- Installer compatibility status (manual input ok for MVP; automated later)

D) Alerting (Alertmanager)
Alert rules (initial thresholds; tune after 2 weeks):
1) Signing failures
- Condition: redwood_release_signing_run_total{result="failure", environment="prod"} / total > 0.10 for 30m
- Severity: page (if release window), otherwise ticket
- Annotation: link to dashboard + runbook
2) Signing duration regression
- Condition: p95(redwood_release_signing_duration_seconds) > baseline * 1.5 for 6h OR absolute > 25m for prod
- Severity: ticket (page if blocks release train)
3) Missing signed artifacts for RC/GA tag
- Condition: signing succeeded but publish_total missing for required artifact types within 20m
- Severity: page during release cut
4) Key expiry warnings
- Condition: (redwood_release_signing_key_not_after_timestamp - now) < 30d (warning), < 14d (ticket), < 7d (page)
- Severity mapping as above
5) Trust store staleness
- Condition: trust_store_age_seconds > 30d (ticket) or > 45d (page)

Owners / on-call routing
- Primary owner: SRE (Sean Gallagher)
- Secondary: Release Engineering (Connor O'Brien)
- For key-related alerts: Security Eng consult (Hanae Suzuki / Marcus Lin) via tag in alert annotations

Runbook updates

- Include:
  - Common failure modes + how to distinguish (HSM/KMS errors vs canonicalization mismatch vs publish failure)
  - When to re-run vs when to stop the train
  - How to collect evidence for audit/compliance (links to key ceremony evidence doc, if applicable)

Implementation tasks
- [ ] Define final metric names/labels with Release Eng + Build/CI (Sahana Iyer) (avoid cardinality traps)
- [ ] Add build-metrics emission in signing workflow steps (success/fail + durations)
- [ ] Add scheduled job to emit key expiry and trust-store metrics
- [ ] Create Grafana dashboard in "Release Engineering" folder and add links in release checklist
- [ ] Add alert rules + routing; verify in staging with synthetic failures
- [ ] Document runbook and link from all alerts

Risks / open questions

- Publish detection requires a reliable source of truth for artifact existence (e.g., object storage index or release manifest). For MVP, query the release artifact registry API if available.
