title: Alerting for quality regressions + scoring signal lag (shadow traffic)
preview:
Problem
- We are adding “shadow traffic” quality scoring during rollouts. Beta customers + internal dogfood need confidence that:
  (1) we will detect statistically meaningful quality regressions in candidate variants
  (2) we will detect when the scoring pipeline is missing/lagging (so dashboards don’t silently go stale)
  (3) we will do this without blowing up cardinality or paging on noise.

Scope
- Define, implement, and validate alert rules for:
  A) Quality regression detection (control vs shadow variant deltas)
  B) Missing/lagging scoring signals (ingestion lag, queue backlog, scoring worker errors)
  C) “Bad data” conditions that make quality deltas untrustworthy (insufficient samples, high drop rate, shadow-target errors)

Non-goals
- Auto-rollback integration (tracked separately). Alerts should feed rollout decisions but not execute them in this ticket.
- Per-prompt / per-customer drilldown alerting (too high cardinality). Alerts should be route/experiment scoped with safe bucketing.

Alerting principles
- Fail-open: alerts must never impact the user path.
- Cardinality-safe: only alert on approved dimensions (tenant tier, route_id bucket, experiment_id, control_model_id, shadow_model_id, metric_name).
- Statistical hygiene:
  - Prefer delta + confidence interval (CI) or minimum sample size gates.
  - Avoid paging on early low-N volatility.
  - Separate “quality regression” from “pipeline unhealthy” so oncall knows whether the problem is model quality vs infra.

Proposed alert set (v1)

1) Quality regression alerts (advisory -> page policy TBD)
- Trigger condition: quality_delta (shadow - control) below threshold for sustained window AND min_samples met.
- Example metrics (v1 bundle):
  - format_adherence_rate_delta
  - refusal_rate_delta
  - instruction_following_score_delta (model-graded)
  - safety_flag_rate_delta (heuristic + model-graded)
- Required gates:
  - min_scored_pairs >= 500 (configurable per metric; use higher for low-base-rate metrics)
  - scoring_freshness < 10m (see “signal freshness” alerts)
  - shadow_pair_drop_rate < 5% (otherwise surface as pipeline degradation)
- Severity:
  - Sev2 (ticket) by default for beta; Sev1 (page) only for “hard regressions” during active rollout windows (guarded by rollout_active=1) after SRE sign-off.
- Initial thresholds (subject to calibration; see Selene/Sana notes):
  - format_adherence_rate_delta <= -0.015 for 30m
  - refusal_rate_delta >= +0.010 for 30m (increase in refusals)
  - instruction_following_score_delta <= -0.10 for 60m
  - safety_flag_rate_delta >= +0.005 for 60m

2) Scoring pipeline lag / missing signal (paging)
- Purpose: dashboards + rollout quality gates are useless if scoring is stale.
- Alerts:
  - ShadowScoringLagHigh: p95 time from shadow_pair_ingested -> score_written > 15m for 20m (Sev2)
  - ShadowScoringLagCritical: p95 > 45m for 15m (Sev1 page)
  - ShadowScoringNoResults: scored_pairs_rate == 0 while shadow_pairs_rate > 0 for 15m (Sev1 page) [guard: only for enabled tenants / active experiments]
  - ShadowQueueBacklogHigh: queue_depth > capacity model threshold for 20m (Sev2)
  - ShadowQueueBacklogCritical: queue_depth growing + worker_utilization pegged for 15m (Sev1)

3) Ingestion / telemetry lag & drops (paging)
- Alerts:
  - ShadowPairIngestionLagHigh: ingestion_lag_seconds p95 > 5m for 20m (Sev2)
  - ShadowPairIngestionLagCritical: p95 > 20m for 15m (Sev1)
  - ShadowPairDropRateHigh: dropped_pairs / total_pairs > 2% for 30m (Sev2)
  - ShadowPairDropRateCritical: > 5% for 15m (Sev1)

4) Shadow-target health degradation (ticket)
- Alerts:
  - ShadowTargetErrorRateHigh: shadow_target_5xx_rate > 2% for 30m (Sev2)
  - ShadowTargetTimeoutRateHigh: shadow_target_timeout_rate > 1% for 30m (Sev2)
- Note: should not page unless it threatens shared infra (circuit breaker should clamp). Primary purpose is to inform experiment owners that data quality is degraded.

Implementation plan
- Add alert rules + recording rules to observability-pack:
  - Compute scored_pairs_rate, unscored_pairs_rate, scoring_lag_seconds, ingestion_lag_seconds
  - Compute CI where available (or proxy with Wilson interval for rates); if CI infra is too heavy, use min_samples gating + rolling windows in v1.
- Add routing rules + ownership:
  - Pipeline lag/ingestion lag -> page Eng Oncall (Shadow / Observability)
  - Quality regression -> ticket to #shadow-traffic-quality + notify rollout owner; page only if rollout_active=1 and “hard regression” threshold met.
- Add runbook links to alerts:
  - Enable/disable kill switch for scoring workers
  - Verify event ingestion + cardinality limits
  - Check backpressure/circuit breaker state
  - Steps to annotate dashboards + communicate to beta customers when metrics are stale

Acceptance criteria
- Alerts exist in staging + prod with documented thresholds and owners.
- Alert rules have:
  - tested PromQL/queries
  - links to runbook + dashboards
  - safe label set (no tenant_id, prompt_id, request_fingerprint)
- Backtest on dogfood data (last 7 days):
  - <= 2 false pages/week for lag alerts
  - Quality regression alerts do not trigger when min_samples not met
- Tabletop exercise with SRE: simulate scoring outage + verify page + runbook resolves within 30 minutes.

Links / references
- Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/shadow-traffic-rollout-and-rollback-runbook.json
- Guardrails/SLOs: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/shadow-traffic-slos-and-guardrails.json
- Observability standard: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/quality-metrics-observability-standard.json
- Related work:
  - ENG-5404 Shadow telemetry events v1
  - ENG-5405 Async scoring pipeline
  - ENG-5410 Cardinality guardrails
  - ENG-5415 Backpressure/circuit breakers
  - ENG-5416 Internal dogfood enablement

Open questions
- Should we gate “Quality regression Sev1 page” behind an explicit per-rollout toggle (e.g., quality_alerts_enforce=true) for beta?
- Minimum sample sizes per metric: confirm with Sana/Selene based on base rates + power notes.
- How to handle rubric version changes mid-experiment (avoid false regression due to rubric drift): likely require rubric_version pinned per experiment and included in aggregates; confirm dimensions are safe.

title: Audit log entries for Shadow Experiments + shadow policy changes (with RBAC updates)
preview:
Background
Shadow traffic introduces a new set of control-plane and Console actions (creating/updating Shadow Experiments, attaching shadow policies to routes/rollouts, tuning sampling, targets, and retention/redaction knobs). For Dedicated/Private and regulated customers, we need a complete audit trail of who changed what, when, and from where.

This ticket adds audit log entries for:
1) Shadow Experiment lifecycle actions (Console backend)
2) Shadow policy lifecycle + attach/detach changes (control plane / route config)
3) Permission model updates so the right roles can manage these settings and the right roles can view the audit trail

Goals
- Ensure every security-relevant mutation is written to the audit log service with consistent event naming.
- Provide “before/after” change context that is useful for investigations without storing prompts/PII or high-cardinality payloads.
- Ensure audit log visibility aligns with RBAC expectations for Hosted/Dedicated/Private, and supports export to customer SIEM in Private.

Non-goals
- Auditing scoring worker internals (queue retries, rubric execution). We only audit control-plane mutations.
- Logging per-request shadowing outcomes (that is telemetry/metrics, not audit logs).

Scope
A) Shadow Experiment audit events (Console)
Emit audit logs for the following actions:
- shadow_experiment.created
- shadow_experiment.updated
- shadow_experiment.deleted
- shadow_experiment.paused (optional if supported in beta)
- shadow_experiment.resumed (optional if supported in beta)

Fields to include (payload must be cardinality-safe and redacted):
- tenant_id / org_id
- actor_user_id, actor_email (if available), actor_role
- actor_auth_type (sso/api_key) if present
- request_id / trace_id
- resource_type=shadow_experiment, resource_id
- route_id (if experiment is route-scoped)
- rollout_id / experiment_id linkage if attached
- change_summary (human-readable, e.g., “sampling: 1% -> 5%; target added: model=rw-llama3-70b-q4”)
- changed_fields (comma-separated allowlist)

Explicitly DO NOT include in audit payload:
- raw prompts, contexts, tool arguments, model outputs
- full header dumps
- unhashed customer end-user identifiers

B) Shadow policy + route attachment audit events (Control plane)
Emit audit logs for:
- shadow_policy.created
- shadow_policy.updated
- shadow_policy.deleted
- route.shadow_policy_attached
- route.shadow_policy_detached

For route attachment/detachment, include:
- route_id
- shadow_policy_id
- effective_sampling_percent
- shadow_targets_count
- fail_open_enabled (bool)
- timeout_ms (bucketed/rounded)

C) Permission model updates
Introduce/confirm the following permissions and wire them to existing roles:
- shadow_experiments:read
- shadow_experiments:write
- shadow_policies:read
- shadow_policies:write
- audit_logs:read (existing; ensure Shadow events are visible under this permission)

Defaults (beta):
- Org Admin / Platform Admin: read/write on experiments + policies
- Security Admin: read audit logs; no write to experiments/policies by default
- Developer: read-only experiments/policies unless explicitly granted

Also ensure API keys inherit the same permission checks as Console users (no bypass).

Implementation notes
- Use existing AuditLog client in Console backend and Control Plane service.
- Event naming must match audit log taxonomy (lowercase, dot-separated), and resources must map to existing resource types so UI filtering works.
- “Before/after” should be implemented as an allowlisted diff. If the audit log service expects JSON, we still only pass allowlisted, pre-redacted values (e.g., sampling_percent, model_id, rubric_version, retention_mode). Avoid embedding entire config objects.
- Ensure idempotency: repeated PUT with no material changes should not emit an audit event.
- For policy updates, capture the actor in control plane context (propagate actor principal from Console request through service-to-service calls).
- Private deployments: verify audit events are available to export pipeline (SIEM connector) without schema changes.

Acceptance criteria
- Creating a Shadow Experiment via Console emits shadow_experiment.created with correct tenant/actor/resource fields.
- Updating an experiment (sampling %, shadow targets, rubric version, attach/detach rollout) emits shadow_experiment.updated with allowlisted changed fields.
- Deleting an experiment emits shadow_experiment.deleted.
- Creating/updating/deleting a shadow policy emits corresponding shadow_policy.* events.
- Attaching/detaching a shadow policy to a route emits route.shadow_policy_attached / route.shadow_policy_detached.
- Users without shadow_experiments:write or shadow_policies:write cannot mutate config (API + Console enforced), and failed attempts do not create audit entries (but do produce standard auth logs).
- Audit log UI/API can filter to these events and displays a readable change summary.
- Unit tests added for event emission + payload redaction rules.
- Integration test (or smoke test checklist) validates end-to-end: Console action -> control plane mutation -> audit record present.

Rollout plan
- Ship behind feature flag: shadow_audit_logs_v1.
- Enable for internal dogfood tenants first; verify volume and correctness.
- Enable for Dedicated beta tenants after Security sign-off.

Risks / open questions
- Actor attribution across service boundaries: ensure we do not lose the initiating principal when Console calls control plane.
- Audit payload size limits: must keep change context small; prefer summaries + changed_fields.
- Role mapping: confirm whether Security Admin should have write access for emergency disable of shadow policies (leaning no; keep separation of duties).

References
- Project: Shadow traffic tool for eval metrics
- Related: ENG-5407 (Console APIs), ENG-5401 (policy schema), ENG-5413 (rollouts integration)
- PR implementation: PR-24966
- Stakeholders: Marcus Lin (Security), Dylan Brooks (Console), Ethan Park / Benji Okafor (control plane)

Fields to include (payload must be cardinality-safe and redacted):
- tenant_id / org_id
- actor_user_id, actor_email (if available), actor_role
- actor_auth_type (sso/api_key) if present
- request_id / trace_id
- resource_type=shadow_experiment, resource_id
- route_id (if experiment is route-scoped)
- rollout_id / experiment_id linkage if attached
- change_summary (human-readable, e.g., “sampling: 1% -> 5%; target added: model=rw-llama3-70b-q4”)
- changed_fields (comma-separated allowlist)

Explicitly DO NOT include in audit payload:
- raw prompts, contexts, tool arguments, model outputs
- full header dumps
- unhashed customer end-user identifiers

Emit audit logs for:
- shadow_policy.created
- shadow_policy.updated
- shadow_policy.deleted
- route.shadow_policy_attached
- route.shadow_policy_detached

For route attachment/detachment, include:
- route_id
- shadow_policy_id
- effective_sampling_percent
- shadow_targets_count
- fail_open_enabled (bool)
- timeout_ms (bucketed/rounded)

C) Permission model updates
Introduce/confirm the following permissions and wire them to existing roles:
- shadow_experiments:read
- shadow_experiments:write
- shadow_policies:read
- shadow_policies:write
- audit_logs:read (existing; ensure Shadow events are visible under this permission)

Defaults (beta):
- Org Admin / Platform Admin: read/write on experiments + policies
- Security Admin: read audit logs; no write to experiments/policies by default
- Developer: read-only experiments/policies unless explicitly granted

Also ensure API keys inherit the same permission checks as Console users (no bypass).

- Use existing AuditLog client in Console backend and Control Plane service.
- Event naming must match audit log taxonomy (lowercase, dot-separated), and resources must map to existing resource types so UI filtering works.
- “Before/after” should be implemented as an allowlisted diff. If the audit log service expects JSON, we still only pass allowlisted, pre-redacted values (e.g., sampling_percent, model_id, rubric_version, retention_mode). Avoid embedding entire config objects.
- Ensure idempotency: repeated PUT with no material changes should not emit an audit event.
- For policy updates, capture the actor in control plane context (propagate actor principal from Console request through service-to-service calls).
- Private deployments: verify audit events are available to export pipeline (SIEM connector) without schema changes.

Acceptance criteria
- Creating a Shadow Experiment via Console emits shadow_experiment.created with correct tenant/actor/resource fields.
- Updating an experiment (sampling %, shadow targets, rubric version, attach/detach rollout) emits shadow_experiment.updated with allowlisted changed fields.
- Deleting an experiment emits shadow_experiment.deleted.
- Creating/updating/deleting a shadow policy emits corresponding shadow_policy.* events.
- Attaching/detaching a shadow policy to a route emits route.shadow_policy_attached / route.shadow_policy_detached.
- Users without shadow_experiments:write or shadow_policies:write cannot mutate config (API + Console enforced), and failed attempts do not create audit entries (but do produce standard auth logs).
- Audit log UI/API can filter to these events and displays a readable change summary.
- Unit tests added for event emission + payload redaction rules.
- Integration test (or smoke test checklist) validates end-to-end: Console action -> control plane mutation -> audit record present.

Rollout plan
- Ship behind feature flag: shadow_audit_logs_v1.
- Enable for internal dogfood tenants first; verify volume and correctness.
- Enable for Dedicated beta tenants after Security sign-off.

Risks / open questions
- Actor attribution across service boundaries: ensure we do not lose the initiating principal when Console calls control plane.
- Audit payload size limits: must keep change context small; prefer summaries + changed_fields.
- Role mapping: confirm whether Security Admin should have write access for emergency disable of shadow policies (leaning no; keep separation of duties).

References
- Project: Shadow traffic tool for eval metrics
- Related: ENG-5407 (Console APIs), ENG-5401 (policy schema), ENG-5413 (rollouts integration)
- PR implementation: PR-24966
- Stakeholders: Marcus Lin (Security), Dylan Brooks (Console), Ethan Park / Benji Okafor (control plane)

title: Backpressure + circuit breaking for shadow traffic (fail-open + graceful degradation)
preview:
Context
Shadow traffic must be strictly fail-open: if shadow target or scoring/ingestion is unhealthy, the user (control) request path must remain unaffected (latency/throughput/errors). During dogfood we saw scoring queue lag (see Slack incident thread: sources/slack/incidents/1768180020-shadow-scoring-queue-backlog.json). We need explicit backpressure and circuit breaking across (1) routing mirroring, (2) telemetry ingestion, and (3) scoring pipeline so we degrade gracefully instead of amplifying load or causing cardinality/infra alerts.

Problem statement
Today the shadow path will continue attempting mirrors and producing telemetry even when downstream components (shadow target, ingestion, scoring) are saturated. This can:
- increase CPU/network overhead on the data plane
- increase metrics/telemetry load when pipelines are already behind
- create delayed/incorrect dashboards (stale deltas) with poor operator visibility
- lead to cascading failures (queue growth -> worker saturation -> further lag)

Goals
- Enforce fail-open guarantees: shadow path failures never block/slow the control response.
- Add explicit backpressure at each hop (mirror -> emit -> ingest -> score) with clear shedding behavior.
- Introduce circuit breakers that automatically stop shadowing when health signals indicate risk.
- Provide observable signals (metrics + logs) to debug why shadow is not running (dropped due to breaker, quota, lag, etc.).
- Integrate with feature flags + kill switches so oncall can disable quickly.

Non-goals
- Perfect delivery of all shadow samples (best-effort only).
- Customer-configurable circuit breaker logic in v1 (we ship sane defaults; later expose knobs).

Proposed design (v1)
1) Data plane mirroring backpressure (inline)
- Hard per-request budgets:
  - max shadow time budget: 150ms (configurable) for establishing request + first token/ack (streaming uses separate semantics per ENG-5414).
  - strict concurrency cap per instance for shadow outbound requests (e.g., semaphore).
  - strict memory cap for buffering shadow request bodies (avoid large prompts causing buffering spikes).
- If budgets are exceeded, drop shadow attempt with reason code; continue control path unaffected.
- Emit a single lightweight drop metric and sample log (rate-limited) rather than full telemetry.

2) Circuit breaker: shadow target health
- Breaker inputs:
  - rolling error rate to shadow target (5xx/timeout) over 1m/5m windows
  - p95 shadow target latency over 1m
  - local concurrency saturation (shadow semaphore at >90% for >N seconds)
- Behavior:
  - OPEN: stop attempting mirroring for that shadow target (still allow control path)
  - HALF_OPEN: allow limited probes (e.g., 1% of eligible samples) to test recovery
  - CLOSED: normal mirroring
- Scope: breaker keyed by (route_id, shadow_target_id, region) to avoid global impact.
- Hysteresis and minimum sample counts to avoid flapping.

3) Telemetry emission shedding
- When breaker is OPEN or when local backpressure triggers, emit only:
  - counter: shadow_mirror_dropped_total{reason=..., target=...}
  - gauge: shadow_breaker_state{target=...}
- Avoid emitting per-request rich events (shadow pair artifacts) when downstream ingestion is unhealthy.
- Use sampling + rate limits for logs to prevent log storms.

4) Ingestion/scoring pipeline backpressure
- Inputs:
  - scoring queue lag seconds (age of oldest message)
  - queue depth
  - worker saturation (utilization)
  - ingestion processing lag (Kafka/stream lag)
- Behavior:
  - When lag exceeds threshold (e.g., >10m), automatically reduce shadow sampling rate at the policy-enforcement layer to a minimum floor (e.g., clamp to 0.1% or 0% depending on severity).
  - When lag exceeds critical threshold (e.g., >30m), set a global “scoring degraded” flag that:
    - turns off rich event emission
    - disables shadow scoring jobs creation (store minimal metadata only)
- Note: rate clamp must preserve deterministic bucketing semantics (ENG-5403): implement “effective_sampling_rate” applied after bucketing decision so a request remains eligible but may be dropped uniformly by second-stage Bernoulli.

5) Operator controls
- Feature flags:
  - shadow_mirroring_enabled (global)
  - shadow_scoring_enabled (global)
  - shadow_rich_telemetry_enabled (global)
- Kill switch surfaced in internal ops (and available to SRE oncall): immediate OPEN breakers + disable mirroring.

Metrics / observability (must-have)
- shadow_mirror_attempted_total{route_id, target_id, region}
- shadow_mirror_success_total{...}
- shadow_mirror_dropped_total{reason, ...} reasons:
  - breaker_open
  - local_concurrency_limit
  - shadow_timeout
  - request_too_large
  - telemetry_shed
  - scoring_degraded
- shadow_breaker_state{state, ...} (0/1 gauges or enum via labels; keep label cardinality bounded)
- shadow_scoring_queue_lag_seconds (gauge)
- shadow_ingestion_lag_seconds (gauge)
- shadow_effective_sampling_rate (gauge, per route target; bounded)
- Dashboards: add panels to existing “Quality delta” dashboard for breaker state + dropped reasons (ref: observability-pack/pr-391-add-quality-delta-dashboards.json)

Acceptance criteria
- Under simulated scoring outage (workers down, queue lag increasing), control path p50/p99 latency delta remains within SLO budget (see shadow SLO doc) and shadow mirrors are automatically reduced/stopped within 5 minutes.
- Under shadow target failure (timeouts/5xx), breaker opens and drop reason counts match expected behavior; no user-visible error increase.
- No metric-cardinality regressions (route_id/target_id must be bounded; no prompt_id/customer_id labels).
- Clear operator visibility: oncall can answer “are we shadowing?” and “why not?” from dashboards.
- Verified via load test suite (ENG-5412) and dogfood follow-up.

Implementation plan
- Add local concurrency limiter + per-request shadow timeout in routing mirror path (ties to ENG-5402 implementation).
- Implement breaker state machine + persistence TTL in routing service (in-memory with periodic reset; no durable storage required for v1).
- Add pipeline health signals to control plane / policy service so effective sampling rate can be clamped.
- Add telemetry shed path and drop-reason taxonomy.
- Wire feature flags + kill switch endpoints.

Risks / trade-offs
- Risk: breaker keyed too broadly -> reduces shadow coverage. Mitigation: key by route+target+region and use HALF_OPEN probes.
- Risk: sampling clamp breaks deterministic comparisons if implemented incorrectly. Mitigation: clamp after bucketing but before mirror attempt, and record effective_sampling_rate in aggregates.
- Risk: metrics could still be high volume. Mitigation: emit only counters/gauges with bounded labels; sample logs.

References / related work
- ENG-5402: routing mirroring implementation
- ENG-5405: scoring queue + workers
- ENG-5414: streaming shadowing semantics
- ENG-5410: cardinality guardrails
- PRs in flight/landed:
  - sources/github/redwood/pr-24895-shadow-pipeline-backpressure-and-circuit-breaker.json (core breaker + lag-based shed)
  - sources/github/redwood/pr-24833-routing-mirror-sampled-requests-fail-open.json (timeouts + fail-open skeleton)
- Incident learnings:
  - sources/slack/incidents/1768180020-shadow-scoring-queue-backlog.json

Open questions
- What should be the default thresholds for OPEN/HALF_OPEN transitions for Dedicated vs Hosted? Proposal: same defaults, but Dedicated can raise max sampling only if they opt in and have capacity headroom.
- Do we clamp sampling at policy layer (control plane) or at data plane only? Proposal: policy layer sets effective_sampling_rate; data plane enforces local budgets regardless.

Test plan
- Unit tests: breaker transitions, drop reason mapping, concurrency limiter.
- Integration tests: simulated shadow target 5xx/timeouts; verify breaker open and control path unaffected.
- Load tests: run perf-canary suite with scoring workers disabled; confirm mirroring reduces and no p99 regression.

Rollout plan
- Internal tenants only behind feature flag.
- Enable in one region for dogfood; monitor breaker panels and control path SLOs.
- Expand to beta Dedicated tenants after SRE sign-off + runbook updates.

title: Regional failover automation upgrade (Hosted API + Dedicated) — umbrella
preview:
## Context
Today, regional failover is a mix of manual actions and partially automated routing policies. Detection can be noisy (false positives), real regional impairment can be slow to identify, and end-to-end safety validation is hard.

This umbrella tracks the Reliability Program work to:
- Improve trigger signal pipeline (reachability, error burn, tail latency burn, capacity headroom, network symptoms)
- Add guardrails + deterministic policy evaluation (avoid flapping/oscillation)
- Validate via controlled failover tests (“game days”) with measurable SLO outcomes
- Improve observability so on-call can quickly distinguish region impairment vs local service issues

## Goals (success criteria)
- Detection + failover time reduced vs baseline (target: automated recommendation within minutes; execution gated by safety limits/optional approval hooks)
- Fewer false-positive regional triggers (measured by alert-to-action ratio + post-event audits)
- No cascading overload during failover (gateway retry budgets + load shedding tuned)
- Repeatable game day plan + results, with follow-ups tracked and closed
- Clear audit trail (who/what/why) for automated recommendations, executions, and rollbacks

## Scope
In-scope:
- Hosted API + Dedicated multi-region routing + policy evaluation
- Region health aggregation service + controller
- Circuit breakers/load shedding behavior during failover
- Dashboards/alerts/runbooks
- Incident-bot + status comms hooks
- Controlled tests in staging + production (limited blast radius)

Out of scope:
- Multi-cloud migration
- Rewriting core serving runtime
- Contract/SLA language changes

## Ownership / RACI (high level)
- Program: Sean Gallagher (Program Lead), Rafael Mendes (Delivery Lead), Talia Benson (PgM)
- Failover architecture + guardrails: Miles Thornton
- Routing + policy engine: Mina Haddad (Eng Lead), Ravi Menon (Tech Lead), Jordan Lee (Policy engine owner)
- Control plane integration: Ethan Park
- Capacity signals + autoscaling gates: Vanessa Ortiz, Keiko Yamamoto
- Network reachability signals: Victor Alvarez, Emily Zhao
- Observability: Allison Grant (Lead), Sergio Costa (Dashboards/alerts), Aisha Bello (SLO dashboards)
- Gateway overload/circuit breakers: Peter Holtz
- Tooling integrations (incident-bot): Devon Price
- Runbooks/docs: Tomáš Novák, Kira Thompson, Elliot Price

## Milestones (Q1–Q2 2026)
### Phase 1 — Baseline + requirements (complete)
- Collect current failover behavior + top incident failure modes
- Define trigger hierarchy draft + initial SLO targets

### Phase 2 — Design (in progress)
- ADR-042: trigger signal priority + corroboration + dampening/hold-down timers
- ADR-043: rollback semantics + anti-flap + audit requirements
- Draft runbooks + controlled test plan

### Phase 3 — Build (in progress)
- Region health aggregator service + caching/TTL + integration points
- Failover controller with dry-run mode + optional approval hooks
- Capacity + network reachability inputs wired into decisioning
- SLO-friendly metrics + burn alerts + dashboards + alert annotations
- Gateway timeouts/retry budgets tuned; standardized retry-after responses

### Phase 4 — Validate (planned)
- Staging drills using config presets + simulation endpoint
- Controlled production game day (explicit go/no-go, limited blast radius)
- Success criteria: failover time, 5xx rate, p95/p99 latency, error budget burn, customer impact checks

### Phase 5 — Rollout + hardening (planned)
- Expand enablement to additional routes/models/tiers via feature flags
- Close gaps found in game days; finalize docs/runbooks
- Establish quarterly failover test cadence + automatic reporting

## Key artifacts / links
Confluence / docs:
- sources/confluence/eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/regional-failover-automation-upgrade-overview.json
- sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/multi-region-availability-slo-2026.json
- sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-042-failover-trigger-signal-hierarchy.json
- sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-043-automated-routing-policy-rollback.json
- sources/confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/region-health-service.json
- sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook.json
- sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook.json
- sources/confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/gpu-pool-drain-and-evacuation-procedure.json
- sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/failover-signal-dashboards-spec.json
- sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/controlled-failover-test-plan-q1-2026.json
- sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process/regional-failover-communication-playbook.json
- sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comms/status-page-template-regional-event.json
- sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/incident-response-security/failover-and-security-boundaries.json

Related PRs (tracking only; individual issues own implementation details):
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19810-add-region-health-aggregator-service.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19812-policy-engine-signal-priority.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19814-failover-controller-dry-run.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19815-routing-policy-rollback-safety.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19816-add-multi-region-slo-metrics.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19818-alerts-failover-burn-rate.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19820-api-gateway-fallback-timeout-tuning.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19821-streaming-reconnect-on-redirect.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19823-integrate-capacity-signal-to-routing.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19824-failover-event-audit-log.json

## Notes / open questions
- Final stance for production: approval hooks required for execution, or only for first N weeks? (see ENG-2415/ENG-2416)
- “Do not fail over” scenarios: partial impairments (single AZ), control plane degradation, noisy network symptoms.
- Data residency boundaries for enterprise tiers: ensure automation respects constraints by policy. (coordinate with security guidance)
- Metric label cardinality limits for reason codes + tenant segmentation; keep dashboards useful without exploding TSDB.

## How to use this umbrella
- Keep cross-team milestones + status here.
- Implementation work stays in sub-issues.
- Any scope change requires updating: Goals, Milestones, and Go/No-Go criteria for the next game day.

title: Define latency SLO tiers and acceptance gates for tiered batching perf CI/canary
preview:
Goal
Finalize numeric latency targets for the three customer-facing SLO tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient) and codify pass/fail gates used by the perf suite + perf-canary so we can safely roll out workload-aware batching defaults.

Scope
- Provide concrete numeric targets (p50/p95/p99) per tier for:
  - Non-streaming requests (end-to-end latency)
  - Streaming requests (TTFT and per-token cadence; explicitly call out what we gate on)
- Define acceptance gates for:
  - Benchmark-lab suite runs (pre-merge / nightly)
  - Perf-canary (merge blocker) thresholds and baseline refresh policy
- Specify which model families/hardware/regions are in-scope for gating for runtime-1.22 launch.

Non-goals
- We are not defining customer contractual SLAs here (Product will reference these for messaging, but SRE owns SLA docs).
- We are not choosing batching knob values here (that’s the scheduler/profile work); this is the target + gating contract.

Proposed tier definitions (for runtime perf validation)
Units: milliseconds. All numbers are measured at the runtime boundary (request admitted -> response complete) for non-streaming, and at runtime boundary for streaming TTFT (request admitted -> first token emitted). Unless noted, gates are evaluated at steady-state concurrency (see “Gating workload shape”).

A) Non-streaming (end-to-end latency)
Fast tier
- p50 <= 350ms
- p95 <= 900ms
- p99 <= 1400ms
Standard tier
- p50 <= 500ms
- p95 <= 1400ms
- p99 <= 2200ms
Cost-efficient tier
- p50 <= 700ms
- p95 <= 2200ms
- p99 <= 3500ms

B) Streaming
We gate streaming on TTFT and “decode smoothness” (not strict p95 token-to-token, but a regression guard). Rationale: batching defaults mainly move queueing/prefill behavior; customers mostly feel TTFT + long stalls.

Fast tier (streaming)
- TTFT p50 <= 250ms
- TTFT p95 <= 650ms
- TTFT p99 <= 1100ms
- Stall guard: % of responses with >250ms inter-chunk gap after first token must not increase by >0.5pp vs baseline (measured on streaming scenarios only).

Standard tier (streaming)
- TTFT p50 <= 350ms
- TTFT p95 <= 900ms
- TTFT p99 <= 1500ms
- Stall guard: % with >400ms inter-chunk gap must not increase by >0.5pp vs baseline.

Cost-efficient tier (streaming)
- TTFT p50 <= 450ms
- TTFT p95 <= 1200ms
- TTFT p99 <= 2000ms
- Stall guard: % with >600ms inter-chunk gap must not increase by >0.5pp vs baseline.

Notes / clarifications
- These are runtime targets for the tiered batching defaults feature. The platform will still enforce overload behavior (backpressure/load shed) rather than allowing unbounded tail latency. Where overload mode triggers, we treat results separately (see “Overload handling in gates”).
- If a model family cannot meet fast-tier p99 on a given hardware class without unacceptable throughput collapse, the correct path is: model-family compatibility doc marks “fast tier not supported” for that hardware (and selection logic must emit a reason code and downgrade). This ticket defines the targets and gating expectations, not the compatibility matrix.

Gating workload shape (benchmark methodology contract)
To avoid bikeshedding in each perf PR, we standardize the workload parameters used by CI/canary for these gates:
- Prompt length buckets: short (<=256 tok), medium (257–1024), long (1025–4096)
- Output length: fixed per scenario (e.g., 128 tok decode for chat, 256 for summarization) to reduce variance
- Mix for tier validation:
  - Fast: 60% short, 30% medium, 10% long; streaming-heavy (>=70% streaming)
  - Standard: 40% short, 40% medium, 20% long; mixed streaming (50/50)
  - Cost-efficient: 30% short, 45% medium, 25% long; mostly non-streaming (>=70% non-streaming)
- Concurrency ramps: evaluate at 3 points (low/target/high) per model/hardware to catch queueing thresholds (exact numbers owned by benchmark-lab but must include a “near saturation” point).

What we gate in CI vs canary
1) Pre-merge (benchmark-lab / perf suite)
- Soft gate (non-blocking initially for 2 weeks after enabling new scenarios): report deltas, create auto-ticket if exceeding thresholds.
- After warmup period: blocking gate for fast-tier TTFT p99 and non-streaming p99 only (to avoid blocking on high-variance metrics early).

2) Perf-canary (merge blocker)
Hard gates (must pass to merge for runtime-1.22 branches once enabled):
- Fast tier:
  - Streaming TTFT p99 must not regress by >7% vs baseline (per model/hardware), and must remain under the absolute target above.
  - Non-streaming p99 must not regress by >7% vs baseline and must remain under absolute target.
- Standard tier:
  - Non-streaming p95 must not regress by >8% vs baseline and must remain under absolute target.
- Cost-efficient tier:
  - Throughput guard (primary): tokens/sec must not regress by >5% vs baseline on cost-efficient scenarios.
  - Tail-latency guard (secondary): non-streaming p99 must not regress by >10% vs baseline.

Baseline policy
- Baselines are per (model family, hardware class, scenario tier, streaming flag).
- Baseline refresh requires:
  - Two consecutive nightly runs showing improvement and stable variance
  - Approval from Runtime Scheduling EM (Sofia) + SRE (Sean) to avoid “refreshing away” a regression
  - Linked internal ticket (use INT-* or ENG-18428 follow-up) documenting why refresh is safe.

Overload handling in gates
- If overload mode triggers (admission control / shedding), we do not fail solely on latency inflation; instead:
  - Verify overload mode is entered at the expected concurrency point (no earlier than baseline by >10%)
  - Verify shed rate does not increase by >1pp vs baseline for fast tier scenarios
- Any earlier-than-baseline overload triggering is treated as a regression.

In-scope for runtime-1.22 gating (initial)
Model families (top traffic):
- Llama-3.x 8B/70B chat variants (hosted)
- Mistral-family mid-size chat (hosted)
Hardware classes:
- H100 and A100 for hosted; L4 for a single “edge/cheap” lane (standard + cost-efficient only)
Regions:
- us-east-1, eu-west-1 (primary); add us-west-2 once variance is within bounds.

Deliverables
- Numbers and definitions above are mirrored into the SRE/architecture SLO tier doc:
  sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/latency-slo-tiers-definition-fast-standard-cost-efficient.json
- Perf-canary gating spec to be implemented under:
  sources/github/perf-canary/pr-489-gate-on-fast-tier-p99-and-standard-tier-p95.json
- Benchmark-lab scenarios updated under:
  sources/github/benchmark-lab/pr-612-add-mixed-workload-slo-tier-scenarios.json

Open questions (resolved in comments below)
- Whether to gate streaming on p95 TTFT or p99 TTFT for fast tier (decision: p99, with baseline % regression + absolute target).
- Whether to publish absolute numbers externally or keep as internal gates (decision: publish tier intent and ranges externally; keep p99 absolute values as internal gates initially; PM to update copy accordingly).

Acceptance criteria
- Confluence SLO tier doc updated with numeric targets and explicit measurement definitions.
- Perf-canary gate definitions agreed by Runtime + SRE and tracked for implementation.
- Any model/hardware exceptions documented as compatibility constraints (reason-code-driven downgrade) rather than silently failing fast tier.

Risks
- High variance on p99 in GPU-shared environments could cause false positives. Mitigation: baseline regression threshold + require multiple-run confirmation for baseline updates; canary runs pin to dedicated canary capacity.
- Streaming stall guard metric instrumentation is not fully baked. Mitigation: implement as “delta vs baseline only” initially and do not hard-block on it until metrics are stable.

Owners
- Primary: Sofia Ivanova (Scheduling)
- Review/approval: Sean Gallagher (SRE), Jonas Weber (Applied ML / evals), Marcus Reed (perf suite integration)

title: SRE review: rollout guardrails (rollback triggers, error-budget policy, monitoring checklist) for mixed-workload scheduler
preview:
Purpose
- Final SRE sign-off on guardrails before enabling the short/long prompt-aware scheduler in production (limited canary in us-east, subset of Dedicated pools).
- Define concrete rollback triggers and “stop the line” conditions.
- Confirm dashboards/alerts and on-call coverage for ramp window.

Context
- Mixed workloads (short chat prompts interleaved with long-context prompts) cause head-of-line blocking under current continuous batching selection, inflating user-visible p95.
- The prototype introduces class-based scheduling (short/long) with aging/starvation prevention and per-iteration token budgets.

Artifacts to review
- Confluence: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/hosted-api-tail-latency-slo-guardrails.json
- Confluence: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/scheduler-class-metrics-dashboard-spec.json
- Confluence: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/mixed-workload-scheduler-rollout-runbook.json
- Perf canary job: sources/github/perf-canary/pr-611-runtime-scheduler-canary-job.json
- Runtime metrics PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-24888-scheduler-metrics-per-class.json

Proposed canary scope (for guardrail definitions)
- Region: us-east
- Pools: Hosted API canary slice + 1 Dedicated pool with known mixed-traffic profile (per SUP-19342)
- Config: feature flag ON + conservative defaults (short/long threshold from trace analysis; aging enabled; token budgets enforced)

Rollout guardrails (what SRE is validating)
1) Rollback triggers (automatic or manual)
- Tail latency:
  - Hosted API chat p95 latency: rollback if +10% relative to baseline sustained for 10 minutes (same model + same pool) AND absolute delta >= 150ms.
  - Hosted API p99 latency: rollback if +15% relative sustained for 10 minutes.
  - TTFT p95: rollback if +20% relative sustained for 10 minutes.
- Errors:
  - 5xx rate: rollback if >= 0.5% for 5 minutes OR >= 0.2% for 15 minutes.
  - Timeouts (client-visible): rollback if >= 0.3% for 10 minutes.
- Scheduler health signals:
  - Starvation counter (long class): rollback if > 0 for 5 consecutive minutes OR any single request waits > 60s in queue (guarded by max-wait metric).
  - Queue depth: rollback if long-queue depth grows monotonically for 15 minutes without corresponding throughput increase (suggests aging/budget mis-tuned).
  - Token budget overrun: rollback if budget_overrun_rate > 1% of iterations for 10 minutes (indicates policy instability / pathologically long-prefill dominance).
- Resource/infra signals:
  - GPU OOM / engine restart rate: rollback if > 2 restarts per pool in 15 minutes.
  - Latency vs utilization mismatch: if p95 worsens while utilization decreases materially (>10% drop) for 15 minutes, treat as strong regression signal (possible kernel selection thrash).

2) Error budget policy during canary
- Canary runs only if current region error budget is healthy (no ongoing burn).
- If burn rate exceeds 2x budget for 30 minutes during ramp window, rollback regardless of latency improvements.
- If burn rate exceeds 4x budget for 10 minutes, immediate rollback.
- Any SEV2+ incident attributed to scheduler behavior: immediate rollback + freeze further rollout until postmortem action items are queued.

3) Ramp schedule constraints (SRE requirements)
- Step function ramps only (no continuous % drift): 1% -> 5% -> 10% -> 25% (stop at 25% for at least 24 hours unless explicitly approved).
- Minimum soak time per step: 45 minutes (Hosted) and 2 hours (Dedicated) unless rollback triggers fire.
- Changes must be reversible via a single feature flag flip (no redeploy required for rollback).
- Only one knob change per soak interval (avoid confounding): either threshold OR budgets OR aging parameters.

4) Monitoring checklist (must be green before ramp)
Dashboards (must be open + pinned during ramp)
- Scheduler class dashboard (per pool/model):
  - queue_depth{class=short,long}
  - time_in_queue_p50/p95{class=*}
  - dispatch_share{class=*}
  - starvation_counter{class=long}
  - token_budget_overruns{class=*}
- Standard inference dashboards:
  - TTFT p50/p95/p99
  - end-to-end latency p50/p95/p99
  - throughput (req/s, tok/s)
  - error rate 4xx/5xx
  - engine restarts / OOM events

Alerts (must exist + route correctly)
- New scheduler alerts (pager/notification): starvation, long queue max-wait, budget overrun
- Existing: p95/p99 regression, 5xx spike, timeout spike
- Routing: #eng-sre + #eng-runtime for notifications; paging to SRE on-call for any rollback trigger

Ownership / on-call coverage
- Ramp commander: Rafael Mendes
- Runtime on point: Lena Fischer (backup: Marcus Reed)
- SRE on point: Aisha Bello (backup: Sean Gallagher)
- Release coordination: Connor O’Brien

Open questions for SRE to answer in this ticket
- Are the p95/p99 and TTFT rollback thresholds acceptable given current SLO buffer in us-east?
- Should long-class max-wait be 60s or 45s for rollback?
- Are the burn-rate gates aligned with our current error-budget policy for Hosted vs Dedicated pools?
- Do we require an explicit “hold” stage at 10% until next business day?

Acceptance criteria
- SRE signs off on rollback triggers and ramp constraints.
- Alerts exist (or are explicitly waived for canary) and are tested (synthetic fire or known-good test path).
- Monitoring checklist is finalized and linked from the rollout runbook.
- Named owners confirm coverage for the planned ramp window.

Notes
- This ticket is a gating item for ENG-2470 (production canary enablement).

title: Add KMS health dashboards + alerts (latency/error rate) and tune paging thresholds
preview:
We need first-class dependency health visibility for KMS across Hosted/Dedicated/Private so on-call can quickly answer:
- Are we failing because KMS is down / throttling / permission misconfigured?
- Is latency creeping up enough to trip request SLOs or trigger retry amplification?
- Are we in degrade-mode / circuit-open and what is the blast radius?

This ticket ships:
1) Dashboards for KMS calls: latency, error rate (by canonical taxonomy), throttling, timeouts, circuit breaker state.
2) Alerts with sane defaults and environment-aware routing (page only for prod; notify-only for staging).
3) Threshold tuning pass to reduce noise during rollout (see eng-sre thread about noisy latency alerts).

Dashboard and alerting should align with the standardized metric names/tags introduced by the KMS wrapper work (ENG-4822/4823) and be linked from the decrypt failures runbook.

Non-goals: building a full dependency SLO framework. This is targeted KMS observability for this quarter’s KMS hardening initiative.

Acceptance criteria:
- Dashboard available in the Observability Pack (Grafana) with per-env + per-region breakdowns.
- Panels include (at minimum):
  - KMS request rate (req/s) split by operation (Decrypt/Encrypt/GenerateDataKey/GetKeyMetadata)
  - Latency p50/p95/p99 with caps/annotation for timeout config
  - Error rate split by canonical category (AccessDenied, NotFound, Throttling, Timeout, InvalidCiphertext, Unknown)
  - Throttling count + retry-attempt histogram (or retries per request)
  - Circuit breaker state (open/half-open/closed) + trips/min
  - Degrade-mode rate (requests served via cached material / fallback mode when applicable)
- Alerts:
  - Page SRE on-call only for prod + customer-facing regions.
  - Warning (non-page) for staging/regional canaries.
  - Alert annotations include top tags: env, region, service, operation, error_category.
- Alert tuning documented (what changed, why, and expected behavior).

Proposed default thresholds (initial, tune after 1 week):
- KMS error rate (critical/page): > 1.0% over 10m, AND > 50 errors over 10m (avoid low-volume noise)
- KMS timeout rate (critical/page): > 0.3% over 10m, AND p95 latency > 300ms over 10m
- KMS throttling (warning): > 0.5% over 15m OR > 200 throttles over 15m (page only if sustained > 30m)
- KMS latency (warning): p95 > 250ms over 15m (notify-only; do not page unless paired with elevated 5xx)
- Circuit breaker open (critical/page): breaker_open == 1 for > 5m in any prod region, or trips/min > 2 sustained 10m

Routing:
- Critical/page -> #eng-oncall + PagerDuty (SRE)
- Warning -> #eng-sre (no page)
- Info -> #eng-platform (during rollout only)

Links / implementation:
- Observability pack PR will include new dashboards + alert rules.
- Ensure dashboards include links to runbook queries and the KMS decrypt failures runbook.

Rollout plan:
- Ship dashboards first (no alerts) to validate metric names/labels.
- Enable alerts in staging/canary for 48h.
- Enable prod warning alerts, observe noise.
- Enable prod paging alerts after tuning and SRE sign-off.

Notes:
- This ticket is directly driven by the KMS timeout spike in us-east and near-miss retry amplification. We need early signals before 5xx rises.
- Ensure metric naming matches the final namespacing decision (see eng-sre metric consistency thread).

title: Launch-week monitoring + rate-limit guardrails for public benchmark landing page
preview:
Context
The benchmark report launch is expected to drive bursty traffic to the public landing page and interactive table (JSON results feed). We need launch-week observability, alerting, and guardrails (rate limits + cache protections) to avoid: (a) marketing site latency/timeouts, (b) cache miss storms, (c) runaway origin load on the benchmark results service, (d) elevated 429s/5xx that degrade the public narrative.

Goals
- Ship a launch dashboard for real-time monitoring (site + results feed + CDN).
- Add pragmatic guardrails: rate limits, circuit breakers, and caching defaults that survive traffic spikes.
- Define an on-call / escalation path + runbook for launch day.
- Validate via pre-launch load test and a controlled cache-warm workflow.

Non-goals
- Re-architect the results service (keep changes minimal; we can iterate after launch).
- “Perfect” DDoS protection beyond existing WAF/CDN (just ensure sane config + visibility).

Success criteria / acceptance
1) Dashboards
- Single “Launch: Benchmark Page” dashboard covering:
  - Marketing site p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate, and top routes
  - Benchmark results JSON endpoint: RPS, p95 latency, 4xx/5xx breakdown, cache hit ratio
  - CDN metrics: cache hit %, origin fetch rate, origin errors
  - Backend: CPU/mem for results service, upstream storage latency
  - User journey funnel events (CTA clicks) for sanity checks during peak (best-effort visibility only)

2) Alerts (paging for Sev2+ only)
- Page on: sustained 5xx on benchmark results endpoint, sustained elevated origin latency, and cache hit ratio collapse.
- Non-paging alerts: elevated 429s, elevated marketing-site TTFB, spikes in origin fetches.
- Alert thresholds documented in the runbook.

3) Guardrails
- Confirm CDN cache TTL and stale-while-revalidate for benchmark artifact JSON.
- Add per-IP + global rate limit on the results endpoint (protect origin, avoid making page unusable).
- Add an emergency “static snapshot” fallback path (manual toggle) if the results service is unhealthy.
- Ensure cache warming procedure is documented and tested.

4) Operational readiness
- Runbook includes:
  - How to interpret dashboards
  - Fast mitigations (raise cache TTL, enable serve-stale, temporarily tighten rate limits)
  - Comms template for Marketing/Support
  - Escalation chain (Eng oncall, Web, Infra)

Implementation plan (high level)
A) Monitoring / dashboards (Datadog + CDN console)
- Create dedicated dashboard: “Launch: Benchmark report (public)”
- Add golden signals and top endpoints:
  - GET /benchmarks (landing)
  - GET /benchmarks/data/v* (JSON artifacts)
  - GET /assets/* (PDF + figures)
- Add synthetic checks for the landing page and JSON feed from 3 regions (NA/EU/APAC).

B) Alerting
- Results endpoint:
  - 5xx rate > 1% for 5m (page)
  - p95 latency > 2s for 10m (page)
  - cache hit ratio < 70% for 10m (page)
- Marketing site:
  - p95 TTFB > 1.5s for 10m (slack)
  - 5xx rate > 0.5% for 10m (slack)
- CDN/origin protection:
  - origin fetch rate spike (> 3x baseline for 10m) (slack)

C) Guardrails
- CDN:
  - Default TTL: 10m for versioned JSON artifacts (v1, v2, …)
  - Enable serve-stale: 60m
  - Ensure content is immutable once versioned; do not overwrite artifacts.
- Results service:
  - Per-IP rate limit (soft) and global concurrency cap
  - Return 304 aggressively on If-None-Match/ETag
  - Ensure responses are cacheable with strong ETags
- Fallback:
  - Manual flag to serve last-known-good artifact directly from object storage (bypass compute) if service unhealthy

D) Launch day operations
- Owner: Sean (SRE)
- Backup: Neha (Reliability), Logan (Telemetry)
- “War room” Slack channel + checklist for launch window.

Notes / risks
- Biggest risk is cache miss storm on first-wave traffic (social/email blast) if CDN config is wrong or artifacts are not warmed.
- Need to align with Legal/PMM that any “fallback snapshot” is acceptable as long as the artifact version is published and consistent.

Links
- Related: ENG-18566 (benchmark results service & cache layer)
- Related: ENG-18477 (artifact export pipeline to web)
- Product: PM-826 (interactive benchmark page requirements + analytics)
- Docs PR (for landing page disclaimers): redwood-docs PR-3912 (reference)

title: Burst pools: implement global fleet safety cap + emergency circuit breaker
preview:
Context
We’re introducing Dedicated “burst capacity” via shared Redwood-managed burst pools per region/hardware class. We need a hard, global fleet safety cap + emergency circuit breaker so a burst surge (or a bug/telemetry lag) cannot degrade non-burst tenants or platform-wide SLOs.

This ticket is specifically about: (1) a global cap that limits burst admissions at the pool/fleet level, and (2) a circuit breaker mechanism that can rapidly deny further burst admissions (and optionally shed burst load) when we detect neighbor impact or unsafe saturation.

Problem statement
During beta we observed two failure modes:
- Pool saturation can cascade into tail amplification for nearby workloads (esp. when bursts coincide with long-context spikes).
- Telemetry lag can make admission decisions “optimistic” for a short period, causing oversubscription beyond safe margins.

We need an explicit, owned mechanism that is:
- Fast (sub-minute reaction for safety conditions)
- Deterministic and explainable (clear denial reasons)
- Operator-friendly (single switch + guarded automation)
- Safe-by-default (prefer denying burst over risking neighbor SLO regressions)

Goals
- Enforce a hard global “fleet safety” limit for each burst pool (region + hardware class), independent of per-tenant caps.
- Provide an emergency circuit breaker that flips burst admission to deny (or to “baseline-only”) when safety triggers fire.
- Integrate with routing so burst-denied requests fall back gracefully (baseline capacity, queue/backpressure, or explicit overload response depending on request type).
- Make it observable: metrics, alerting, and annotations that help on-call understand why burst is disabled.
- Support both manual operation (runbook-driven) and automated triggers (SLO regression gate) with conservative defaults.

Non-goals
- Cross-region shedding of baseline Dedicated traffic.
- Complex spot-market behavior or dynamic repricing.
- Customer-specific circuit breakers (shared mechanism; per-tenant caps are separate).

Proposed behavior (high-level)
1) Safety cap definition
- For each pool key (region, hardware_class), define MAX_BURST_GPU_EQUIV and/or MAX_BURST_UTIL_PCT.
- Cap should be “hard”: once exceeded, admission controller returns deny for all burst requests (or denies burst component and permits baseline-only where applicable).

2) Circuit breaker trigger inputs
We will support multiple triggers; any can open the circuit:
- Pool saturation threshold: pool_free_capacity < X for Y seconds OR admitted_burst_util > U% for Y.
- Telemetry freshness: if fleet telemetry staleness > T seconds, fail closed for burst (deny) rather than oversubscribe.
- Neighbor-impact SLO gate: if non-burst p95 latency delta exceeds threshold OR error-rate for protected routes increases beyond gate for N minutes.

3) Circuit breaker modes
- CLOSED: normal burst behavior; admissions constrained by tenant + fairness + pool caps.
- OPEN (deny burst): deny new burst admissions with reason code FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN.
- DEGRADED (optional follow-up): allow burst only for priority tier “gold” up to a smaller cap. (Note: keep this feature-flagged; not required for GA unless we’re confident it won’t create starvation.)

4) Denial semantics
- Admission controller returns a deterministic deny decision with structured reason codes and retry_after_ms hint.
- Routing behavior on deny:
  - Prefer “baseline-only” if request has baseline capacity available.
  - If baseline is saturated too, follow existing overload/backpressure policy (429 for customer-facing API where appropriate; internal workloads may queue).

5) Operator controls
- Manual kill switch:
  - Per pool (region/hw) and global-all-pools switch.
  - Stored in control plane config (audited) with a short propagation path.
- Automatic gate:
  - SLO regression gate can open circuit automatically. Auto-close should require sustained recovery + a cooldown.

Acceptance criteria
- A global cap is enforced in the admission controller for each pool key and can be adjusted via config.
- A circuit breaker can be opened manually within 1 minute and causes burst admissions to be denied across the pool.
- Telemetry freshness check exists; when stale beyond threshold, the system defaults to denying burst (not admitting).
- Routing respects the denial reason and behaves consistently (baseline-only fallback or overload response).
- Observability:
  - Metrics: circuit_open (gauge), opens_total, denials_by_reason, telemetry_staleness_seconds, pool_utilization.
  - Dashboard panel + alert: “Circuit breaker open” with runbook link.
- Runbook updated with:
  - How to identify trigger (saturation vs SLO gate vs telemetry stale)
  - How to open/close circuit breaker
  - How to communicate to Support/CS (expected customer symptoms)

Implementation notes
- Primary owner: Routing SRE + Infra/SRE (Peter), with infra signals from Vanessa/Keiko.
- Implement as part of admission decision pipeline (not routing-only) so we have a single source of truth for “burst allowed”.
- Config and audit events:
  - If we add new config fields or kill switch toggles, ensure they’re audited (ties into existing burst config audit work).
- Determinism:
  - Circuit state should be consistent across admission instances; use shared state in control plane/policy engine or a small strongly-consistent store.
  - If uncertain (can’t fetch state), fail closed for burst.

Rollout plan
- Stage 0: behind feature flag in staging, validate with synthetic burst + induced telemetry lag.
- Stage 1: dogfood on internal tenants in us-east/H100 only; validate alerts aren’t noisy.
- Stage 2: beta pool(s) with SRE on-call awareness and clear rollback path.
- GA gate: required for broad region rollout.

Open questions
- Do we want automatic closing of the circuit breaker, or require manual close only for GA? Proposal: manual close only for GA; auto-close can come post-GA once we have confidence in triggers.
- When circuit opens, do we also shed existing burst load (active in-flight)? Proposal: no preemption for GA; deny new burst + rely on normal load completion. (Shedding/preemption is risky and customer-visible.)

title: Support playbook + standardized denial reason codes for Dedicated Burst admission
preview:
Problem
Support/CS are seeing multiple flavors of "burst denied" behavior during beta (429s, overload, routing fallback, and occasionally silent slowdown). Without standardized denial reason codes and a consistent customer-facing explanation, Support cannot reliably answer:
- Why was my burst denied?
- Is this expected (limits/guardrails) vs. an incident?
- What should we do next (retry/backoff vs. request config change vs. escalate to SRE/Fleet)?

This ticket standardizes the denial reason codes emitted by the Burst Admission Controller and ensures:
1) The reason codes are surfaced consistently through the gateway/routing path.
2) Support tooling exists to explain “why” for a specific request/tenant/time window.
3) A Support playbook exists with customer-ready language, troubleshooting steps, and escalation paths.

Scope
A. Denial reason code taxonomy (v1)
- Define canonical machine codes + short summary + operator notes + customer-facing wording.
- Ensure codes are stable and versioned (don’t repurpose meanings).
- Map codes to expected client behavior (retry vs. don’t retry) and expected HTTP status.

B. Response surfacing & determinism requirements
- Ensure every burst denial includes a reason code that makes it to the customer response (or is visible in Console/usage export for async/batch).
- Ensure routing fallback does not mask burst denials (if fallback happened, surface that burst was denied + fallback applied).
- Ensure reason codes are deterministic for identical policy inputs (policy eval + telemetry snapshot), and when not deterministic, explicitly mark as "unknown/telemetry".

C. Support playbook + internal troubleshooting tooling
- Create a Support-facing guide: how to interpret denial codes, what dashboards to check, what to ask the customer, and escalation criteria.
- Provide a lightweight internal debug path (safe/redacted) to answer: "which guardrail triggered?" including current tenant config and effective limits.

Proposed denial reason codes (v1)
Note: Prefix with "BURST_" for discoverability in logs, metrics labels, and support macros.

1) BURST_NOT_ENABLED
- Meaning: Tenant is not enabled for burst, or burst feature flag off for tenant.
- Expected HTTP: 403 (or 429 if product wants a consistent throttling surface; decision documented elsewhere).
- Customer guidance: "Burst is not enabled for your account in this region/hardware class. Contact your TAM/Support to enable."

2) BURST_REGION_OR_HW_NOT_ALLOWED
- Meaning: Burst is enabled, but this request is routed to a region/hardware class not allow-listed for burst.
- Expected HTTP: 409 (misconfiguration) or 403.
- Customer guidance: "Burst is enabled but not available in this region/hardware class. We can help adjust routing or availability."

3) BURST_BASELINE_ONLY (aka "not a burst request")
- Meaning: Request was served within dedicated baseline; no burst was used.
- Not a denial; used for support clarity in reports/debug output.

4) BURST_CAP_REACHED
- Meaning: Tenant hit configured cap (e.g., max burst GPUs, max burst ratio vs baseline, max burst concurrency).
- Expected HTTP: 429
- Retry: Yes, with backoff; include Retry-After.
- Customer guidance: "You reached your configured burst limit. You can wait for capacity to free up or request a higher burst cap."

5) BURST_COOLDOWN_ACTIVE
- Meaning: Tenant exceeded burst burst-window rules and is in cooldown.
- Expected HTTP: 429
- Retry: Yes, but not immediately; include Retry-After aligned with cooldown remaining.
- Customer guidance: "Burst cooldown is active to protect the fleet. Retry after the cooldown window or reduce spike aggressiveness."

6) BURST_POOL_SATURATED
- Meaning: Burst pool is out of borrowable capacity for this region/hardware class.
- Expected HTTP: 429 (preferred) or 503 if we want to distinguish pool exhaustion vs per-tenant caps.
- Retry: Yes, with exponential backoff + jitter.
- Customer guidance: "Shared burst pool is saturated. We can review peak windows and pre-warm options / capacity planning."

7) BURST_FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN
- Meaning: Global safety cap/circuit breaker opened (protecting non-burst SLOs or fleet health).
- Expected HTTP: 503 (service protection) or 429 with a distinct code.
- Retry: Yes, but expect longer recovery; Support should check incident channel.
- Customer guidance: "Burst is temporarily limited to protect platform reliability."

8) BURST_NEIGHBOR_SLO_GUARDRAIL
- Meaning: SLO regression gate triggered (non-burst p95/p99 latency regression, tail amplification, error rate spike).
- Expected HTTP: 503 or 429 depending on policy.
- Retry: Yes, but may be disabled until gate clears.
- Customer guidance: "Burst temporarily restricted due to reliability guardrails."

9) BURST_FAIRNESS_THROTTLE
- Meaning: Tenant is being throttled by fairness algorithm (token bucket / tiering) even though pool not fully saturated.
- Expected HTTP: 429
- Retry: Yes, backoff recommended.
- Customer guidance: "Burst is shared with fairness controls; throughput may be limited during contention."

10) BURST_ADMISSION_TIMEOUT
- Meaning: Admission controller did not respond within budget; request failed safe.
- Expected HTTP: 503
- Retry: Yes; Support should treat as potential incident if elevated.
- Customer guidance: "Transient control-plane timeout; please retry."

11) BURST_TELEMETRY_LAG_GUARD
- Meaning: Telemetry lag exceeded threshold; admission controller refuses to grant burst to avoid unfairness/over-admission.
- Expected HTTP: 429 or 503
- Retry: Yes; Support should check telemetry lag dashboard.
- Customer guidance: "Temporary capacity signal degradation; retry shortly."

12) BURST_CONFIG_INVALID
- Meaning: Tenant config references invalid limits/tier; admission fails closed.
- Expected HTTP: 500 internally; customer surface should be a 503 with a stable error class.
- Retry: No until fixed.
- Customer guidance: "Configuration error on our side; we are fixing."

Response contract (customer surface)
- Always include a machine-readable code in response, e.g.:
  - JSON error field: "burst_denial_reason": "BURST_POOL_SATURATED"
  - Header: "x-redwood-burst-denial-reason: BURST_POOL_SATURATED"
  - Header: "retry-after" or "x-redwood-retry-after-ms" when applicable.
- Ensure routing logs include: tenant_id, region, hw_class, baseline_alloc, effective_burst_cap, decision, reason_code, fairness_bucket/tier, telemetry_snapshot_ts.
- Ensure metrics label cardinality stays bounded: reason_code is ok; DO NOT label by tenant_id.

Support playbook deliverables (what Support/CS gets)
1) One pager: "Burst denials: what they mean" (customer-safe definitions + recommended actions)
2) Triage checklist:
- Confirm tenant burst enablement + region/hw allowlist
- Check if denial is per-tenant cap vs pool saturation vs fleet circuit breaker
- If cap: confirm configured limits + suggest mitigation (increase cap / smooth traffic / batching)
- If pool saturated: check regional pool saturation dashboard + advise retry/backoff; consider proactive capacity request
- If circuit breaker/SLO gate: check incident channel and SRE notes; do not promise timelines without Fleet/SRE
3) Escalation rubric:
- Page SRE if: BURST_ADMISSION_TIMEOUT spikes, BURST_TELEMETRY_LAG_GUARD sustained >10m, or non-burst p95 regression observed.
- Loop Fleet if: recurring BURST_POOL_SATURATED at predictable times for multiple tenants in same region/hw.
- Loop Product if: repeated BURST_FAIRNESS_THROTTLE complaints from high-tier customers.
4) Canned macros/templates for Support:
- Short explanation per denial code
- Recommended client retry/backoff language
- When to share dashboard screenshots vs internal-only info

Acceptance criteria
- Canonical list of denial reason codes published (internal) and referenced by routing + admission controller + docs.
- Customer-facing error surface includes the reason code for all burst denials (including when fallback is applied).
- Dashboard includes breakdown of denials by reason code (region/hw level).
- Support playbook published and linked from:
  - Runbook: burst pool overload mitigation
  - Jira templates for burst-related tickets
- Support training: 30-min walkthrough with Support + CS; confirm they can answer: "why denied" + "what next".

Out of scope
- Changing pricing/packaging or contractual SLA language (handled in PM/legal threads).
- Cross-region bursting (explicitly out of scope for initial release).

References / related work
- Confluence: Dedicated burst SLOs and alerting
- Confluence: Burst pool overload mitigation runbook
- GitHub PRs referenced in updates (see Activity)
- Support tickets motivating this:
  - SUP-28411 Dedicated burst denials unexpected
  - SUP-28503 429s when burst cap reached
  - SUP-28455 Noisy neighbor latency during burst window

Open questions
- HTTP status mapping: do we standardize all denials as 429 + reason_code, reserving 503 for admission timeouts/circuit breaker? (Prefer consistent 429 for client behavior, but SRE prefers 503 for “platform protection” scenarios.)
- Retry-After: seconds vs ms? Standardize across gateway.
- Should we expose a “denial_category” (TENANT_LIMIT vs POOL_LIMIT vs FLEET_SAFETY) to reduce customer confusion?

title: Incident drill + game day execution for Dedicated Burst Capacity
preview:
Goal
Run a structured game day for Dedicated Burst Capacity before GA readiness gates. Validate that guardrails protect non-burst tenants, that SLO-based gates work, and that on-call has a clear, repeatable mitigation path.

Scope
- Execute scenarios from the game day plan (synthetic bursts + induced failures).
- Capture observations, timelines, and concrete outcomes (what worked / what didn’t).
- Produce follow-up fixes as Linear tickets (with owners) and ensure they are tied to GA checklist.

References
- Confluence: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/burst-capacity-game-day-plan.json
- Runbook draft: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/burst-pool-overload-mitigation-runbook.json
- Dashboards catalog: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/burst-capacity-dashboards.json
- SLOs/alerts: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/dedicated-burst-slos-and-alerting.json

Planned participants
- SRE: Rafael Mendes (facilitator), Sean Gallagher (SLO gate sign-off)
- Platform: Ethan Park, Jordan Lee (policy/determinism), Vikram Singh (data plane)
- Routing: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon
- Infra/Fleet: Keiko Yamamoto (guardrails), Vanessa Ortiz (pool capacity)
- Observability: Allison Grant / Sergio Costa

Environment
- Staging for initial dry run; then canary prod slice (internal workloads only) with feature flags.
- Burst enabled for 2 internal tenants + 1 dedicated test tenant (non-customer), across us-east/H100.

Scenario list (executed)
1) Pool saturation under coordinated burst
- Drive burst traffic to exceed shared pool headroom while keeping baseline dedicated steady.
- Validate: admissions deny w/ correct reason; queue/backpressure behavior matches ADR; circuit breaker does not trip prematurely.

2) Telemetry lag / staleness
- Introduce 60–120s lag in pool saturation signals (simulate metrics pipeline delay).
- Validate: determinism guarantees; no oscillatory admit/deny; fairness not starved due to stale utilization.

3) Admission controller partial outage / elevated latency
- Inject 5xx + 500ms–2s latency on admission controller.
- Validate: routing fallback behavior, timeouts, and that we fail closed vs fail open per policy; verify user-facing errors are consistent.

4) Routing policy mismatch / config propagation delay
- Change tenant burst config mid-test (caps + cooldown) and observe propagation.
- Validate: audit event emitted, control plane reflects change, Console surfaces eventual consistency expectations.

5) Neighbor impact check (non-burst tenant)
- While burst tenant spikes, run steady load on a non-burst tenant.
- Validate: non-burst p95 and tail amplification within protected thresholds; SLO gate triggers if regression crosses threshold.

6) Global safety cap / emergency kill switch
- Force conditions to approach fleet safety cap.
- Validate: global breaker transitions and clear alerting; rollback steps are fast, safe, and verifiable.

Deliverables
- Game day notes + timeline + metrics screenshots (linked in comments).
- Final outcome summary + GA readiness recommendation.
- Follow-up tickets for any gaps.

Notes on expectations
- We are explicitly optimizing for non-burst protection: it’s OK for burst requests to be denied aggressively during safety events.
- Primary success criteria is predictable behavior + clear operator action paths, not maximizing burst admission rate.

title: SIEM audit export: E2E integration tests (Splunk/Datadog/S3) + chaos scenarios + idempotency assertions
preview:
Build and wire up end-to-end integration tests for audit export adapters (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs intake, S3 delivery) using sandbox/stubbed endpoints, and validate delivery semantics (at-least-once), dedup behavior (event_id), checkpointing, backfill windows, and failure handling (retry/backoff, DLQ). Include chaos scenarios to ensure exporter remains stable during downstream throttling/outages and does not violate max-lag safety controls.

Context:
- We explicitly guarantee at-least-once delivery (not exactly-once). E2E tests must prove: (1) exporter can safely retry without dropping events, (2) replay/backfill produces duplicates as expected, and (3) duplicates are detectable via stable event_id for downstream dedup.
- Beta customers are already asking for “missing events” explanations; we need hard, reproducible tests to back runbook guidance and reduce regressions during rapid adapter iteration.

Non-goals:
- Performance benchmarking at production scale (covered by separate load/backpressure experiments).
- Private/on-prem networking patterns (out of beta scope).

Acceptance criteria:
1) CI job executes E2E suite on each PR to audit-log-exporter repo (and nightly against main).
2) Tests cover happy-path delivery for Splunk, Datadog, and S3 with assertions on payload shape (schema v2 required fields) and stable event_id propagation.
3) Chaos tests cover downstream 429 throttling (Splunk), intermittent 5xx (Datadog), and S3 transient failures, verifying retries, backoff, and checkpoint progression.
4) Tests validate idempotency/dedup story: exporter will resend on retry; resulting duplicate event_id is expected and asserted.
5) A failing batch that exceeds retry policy lands in DLQ and can be replayed by operator tooling; replay is verified in E2E.
6) Tests validate “max lag” safety: sustained downstream outage triggers auto-pause (where configured), surfacing status/health correctly.

Implementation notes:
- Use docker-compose harness with:
  - Splunk HEC stub (HTTP server emulating /services/collector behavior, token auth, 429 responses, optional gzip acceptance).
  - Datadog intake stub (validates API key header, returns 403 for scoped/invalid keys, can inject 5xx/slow responses).
  - Localstack (or real AWS sandbox) for S3, including SSE-S3 and SSE-KMS paths (KMS path can be simulated or validated via config contract tests if Localstack KMS insufficient).
- Synthetic audit event generator: deterministic seed; event_id must be stable across retries and replays; include a mix of categories/actions to ensure filter config doesn’t accidentally exclude.
- Keep cardinality under control: labels used in tests should match exporter metrics constraints (no unbounded tag values).

Links:
- GitHub: sources/github/audit-log-exporter/pr-1112-integration-tests-with-splunk-and-datadog-sandboxes.json
- Related incident learnings: downstream 429 → lag spike (see #incidents thread referenced by SRE)
- ADRs: delivery semantics/idempotency; filtering model
- Runbook alignment: audit exporter delivery failures

  - Datadog intake stub (validates API key header, returns 403 for scoped/invalid keys, can inject 5xx/slow responses).
  - Localstack (or real AWS sandbox) for S3, including SSE-S3 and SSE-KMS paths (KMS path can be simulated or validated via config contract tests if Localstack KMS insufficient).
- Synthetic audit event generator: deterministic seed; event_id must be stable across retries and replays; include a mix of categories/actions to ensure filter config doesn’t accidentally exclude.
- Keep cardinality under control: labels used in tests should match exporter metrics constraints (no unbounded tag values).

Links:
- GitHub: sources/github/audit-log-exporter/pr-1112-integration-tests-with-splunk-and-datadog-sandboxes.json
- Related incident learnings: downstream 429 → lag spike (see #incidents thread referenced by SRE)
- ADRs: delivery semantics/idempotency; filtering model
- Runbook alignment: audit exporter delivery failures

title: Auto-stop and rollback canary rollouts on SLO breach (5xx/timeouts/p95 latency)
preview:
Implement automatic stop conditions for Hosted canary rollouts and wire them into the rollout orchestrator.

Scope:
- Evaluate SLO regressions comparing baseline vs canary cohorts:
  - 5xx error rate
  - timeout rate
  - p95 latency regression
- When stop condition trips, orchestrator should either:
  - auto-pause (default for Beta), or
  - auto-rollback (GA default for tenants with guardrail policy enabled), depending on tenant policy + rollout configuration.
- Notify: Console surface "Auto-stopped" reason + event timeline entry; send internal notification (PagerDuty for severe, Slack for non-severe) + optional customer email/webhook (if enabled for tenant).

Non-goals:
- Automated quality gates as a hard blocker (still allow optional manual quality metrics display; no automated gating in this ticket).

Notes:
- Must be safe under partial telemetry outages: avoid silent failures; avoid rollouts getting stuck indefinitely; ensure deterministic behavior on metric fetch timeouts.
- Must be tunable by tier/region without creating metric-cardinality blowups.

Related artifacts:
- PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-28845-auto-stop-rollback-on-slo-breach.json
- Bugfix: sources/github/redwood/pr-28895-fix-rollout-step-stuck-on-metric-timeout.json
- SRE signals doc: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/hosted-rollouts-slo-metrics-and-alerts.json
